text: Surkhet Multiple Campus, Research Management Cell (RMC)   Vol. 2, Issue 1, July 2023 Surkhet Journal Research Management Cell (RMC) Surkhet Multiple Campus, Birendranagar, Surkhet Research Paradigms in English Education: A Brief Overview Dipendra Kumar Khatri Lecturer, Surkhet Multiple Campus dipendraskt2039@gmail.com Abstract The researcher's worldview for the particular investigation is the research paradigm. So t he main objective of this paper is to examine various forms of research paradigms with the library study attempting to link with English education. The description is tried to make clear by linking with examples of selected an issue. For this, the paper starts by introducing a research paradigm as the looking glass to the world. So it is the lens of the researcher through which the study is completed. It was found that positivism is based on objective reality which is most appropriate in nat ural science and post-positivism is the modification (of positivism ) as demands more interaction between the researcher and participants . On the other hand, interpretivism is to understand the issue from an insider's view and constructivism is aimed at social construction. Furthermore, criticalism is to improve the situation of the oppressed , and feminism is purposeful to empower females. Moreover, pragmatism is to g enerate context -based knowledge and post-modernism is to exp lore representational knowledge. In a sense, integralism is to integrate different paradigms within one single research. This paper a lso explores the components of the research where ontology is a study of reality, epistemology is the study of knowledge generation , and methodology is the process of doing the particular research. It implicitly reflects the axiology as the study of value systems. Overviewing the various shifts from traditional to relatively today's research , the specific issue s are selected in connection to English education and suggested to link them contextually. Keywords Observation, positivism, post-modernism, reality, knowledge, integralism Introduction Concept of Research Paradigms The p aradigm is the overall pattern of visioning something. The research paradigm is simply taken as the pattern of investigation : perceiving reality, knowledge, and way of achieving them . A research paradigm is a manner to answer ontological, epistemological, and methodologi

ords Observation, positivism, post-modernism, reality, knowledge, integralism Introduction Concept of Research Paradigms The p aradigm is the overall pattern of visioning something. The research paradigm is simply taken as the pattern of investigation : perceiving reality, knowledge, and way of achieving them . A research paradigm is a manner to answer ontological, epistemological, and methodological questions Surkhet Journal Vol. 2, Issue 1, July 2023, Print ISSN 2362-1230   (Guba & Lincoln, 1994). Paradigms search for what is a reality in that particular issue, how knowledge can be created with the selected issue, what steps are necessary to follow to complete the research task , and what value should be taken into account. The main mechanisms of any research paradigm as mentioned in Killam (2013), are ontology: the study of the researcher's beliefs about the nature of reality, the epistemology: examines the relationship between knowledge and researcher during investigation, methodology: the study of discovering knowledge, and axiology: the study of value system. So, the components of a paradigm are: ontology (study of reality), epistemology (how knowledge can be created), and methodology (why, what, from where, when, and how data is collected and analyzed) and methods are the spec ific techniques and procedures used to collect and analyze data (Scotland, 2012). As the research paradigm is the overall looking -glass researcher, it is full of philosophical models to complete the research. Thus, the research paradigm is the philosophical outline of systematic carrying out research. It can be introduced under the term 'model for searching answers' to the research question. The research paradigm is the view of the whole task which sets the foundation for the whole activities to ho ld in the process of search. According to Willis, (2007, p.8), "A paradigm is thus a comprehensive belief system, world view, or framework that guides research and practice in a field." So a research paradigm is the pattern, mo del, or overall philosophical process of investigation. Various Research Paradigms in English Education Different scho lars have explained various discussions o n paradigms from past to present as found in literature studies. Regarding this, Scotland (2012) illustrated scientific, interpretive, and critical as the major paradigms in educational research. Sim

a research paradigm is the pattern, mo del, or overall philosophical process of investigation. Various Research Paradigms in English Education Different scho lars have explained various discussions o n paradigms from past to present as found in literature studies. Regarding this, Scotland (2012) illustrated scientific, interpretive, and critical as the major paradigms in educational research. Similarly, Taylor (2014) described post -positivism, interpretivism, criticalism, and post -modernism as commonly used in cont emporary social science. To this point, Kumatongo and Muzata, (2021) explained positivism, post -positivism, interpritivism, constructivism, and pragmatism. Using elaborative terminologies T aylor et al. (2012) discussed positivist and post-positivist under 'single paradigm research.' Likewise , interpretive, pragmatism , and post-modernism are within the 'multiple research paradigms' and further they introduce an 'integral perspective in research '. Thus positivism, post- positivism, interpretivism, criticalism, feminism, post-modernism, and integralism are the most discussed paradigms in the literature on research issues related to social science specifically in English education. Although many theoretical explanations are found about the research paradigms, very few practical links are observed in the descriptions and in their implementation. So, theoretical justification in practice is a prerequisite. Then, only application is possible. Thus, illustrating various research paradigms from different sources and attempting to make a linkage between explanation and execution (to English language education) is the main objective of this paper. Methodology This is a descriptive research paper based on the secondary sources of library study . The main objective is to examine v arious paradigms in social science specifically with examp les from English education. After studying the various literatures, the interaction is done with those literatures. To make the concept clear, some examples are given from each of the paradigms with reference to English 22 | Research Paradigms in English Education: A Brief Overview Surkhet Multiple Campus, Research Management Cell (RMC)   education. The philosophical components of each paradigm are also tried to include in th is paper. The conclusion is drawn from the discussion of collected secondary sources. Findi

the concept clear, some examples are given from each of the paradigms with reference to English 22 | Research Paradigms in English Education: A Brief Overview Surkhet Multiple Campus, Research Management Cell (RMC)   education. The philosophical components of each paradigm are also tried to include in th is paper. The conclusion is drawn from the discussion of collected secondary sources. Findings and Discussion Positivism Positivism is based on the view of only one reality: objectivity and suitable to te st hypotheses or theories. The only reality that can be achieved by the application of reason and experiment . As Scotland (2012) has given the title 'scientific paradigm ' with the claim of mostly used in natural science research, and elaborated positivism synonymously within it . Positivism is therefore close to objectivism which argues that reality exists external to the researcher and must be investigated through a rigorous process of scientific inquiry. To explain this concept, Kumatongo and Muzata (2021, p.17) mention, "knowledge is based on experience of senses and can be obtained by observation and experiment." Likewise, Henning, et al. (2004) as cited in Kumatongo and Muzata (2021, p.17), positivism can be the best-used method for science education researchers. To discuss posit ivism, Taylor et al. (2012) explained the term 'single paradigm research' which has objectivity and only one way of knowing the reality. On the basis of the above discussion , i t can be presented that the ontology of this research is (absolute) naïve realism: reality already exists in the world, the epistemology is objectivism : only one reality that should be discovered by observation, and methodology is experimental quantitative (Guba, 1994). For instance, 'Effectiveness of Guided Writing in Teaching Composition' can be a res earch issue to examine with positivism. To investigate this particular issue in English education, I can take the positivism paradigm because I want to experiment with making an assumption that guided writing is effective for developing writing skills. It will be experimented in two groups as mentioned by Taylor and Medina (2013) experimental (or treatment) and control groups which involves quantitative methodology . The test item will be used as the tool to prove the only one objective truth: there is effectiveness of guided writing in teaching c

xperiment with making an assumption that guided writing is effective for developing writing skills. It will be experimented in two groups as mentioned by Taylor and Medina (2013) experimental (or treatment) and control groups which involves quantitative methodology . The test item will be used as the tool to prove the only one objective truth: there is effectiveness of guided writing in teaching composition . In this study, i t has no role of researcher to influence the result, the influence is dependent on cause (guided writing) and effect (composition writing). Post-positivism It is a modified scientific or positivis t method : process, and tools for the social science and follows the same principles but allows more interaction between the researcher and participants (Willis, 2007). The limitations of posi tivism are tried to be reduced by thinking further to understand causes that influence outcomes (Kumatongo & Muzata 2021). Post-positivism is a further improved paradigm to overcome the weakness of posit ivism, to produce objective and generalizable knowledge by quasi - experimental res earch that utilizes treatment. Broadly, Taylor and Medina (2011) mentioned both positivism and post -positivism under the common title 'traditional paradigm'. It can be explained that the ontology of this research is critical realism (Guba, 1994), the epistemology is modified dualist or objective , and the methodology is deductive with modified experimental. For instance, 'Effectiveness of Brainstorming in Teaching Paragraph Writing' can be a research issue to be investigated with the application of post- positivism. Dipendra Kumar Khatri | 23@. Surkhet Journal Vol. 2, Issue 1, July 2023, Print ISSN 2362-1230   To study this particular issue in English education, I can take the post -positivism paradigm because I want to find out the effectiveness of brainstorming techniques in teaching parag raph writing. For this, an experimental group is treated with brainstorming techniques to teach para graph writ ing. Moreover, test items can be developed and additional survey questions can be used as the tool t o collect data. At this point, Creswell, (2008) as cited in Taylor and Medina (2011) mentioned that the post-positivism paradigm uses additional methods such as surveys, qualitative interviewing , and participant observation. The added tools and techniques create more inte

to teach para graph writ ing. Moreover, test items can be developed and additional survey questions can be used as the tool t o collect data. At this point, Creswell, (2008) as cited in Taylor and Medina (2011) mentioned that the post-positivism paradigm uses additional methods such as surveys, qualitative interviewing , and participant observation. The added tools and techniques create more interaction and modification than positivism. Interpretivism Making meaning is the result of various factors. People use perception to interpret what their senses tell them (Al-saadi 2014). As Scotland (2012) wrote reality is not objective, it is subjective perception and interpretation. The ont ological position of interpretivism is multiple realism and knowledge is subjective which is based on real-world phenomena and experiences perceived by different individuals. Kivunja and Kuyini (2017) have discussed within the same heading of interpretivist and constructivist paradigms to generate context-based understanding of people's thoughts, beliefs, and values. The act of interpretation of other's meaning perspective is mentioned by Taylor et al. (2012) and kept under the term multiple research paradigm. Similarly, Taylor and Medina (2013) described under the 'relatively new and humanistic paradigm ' which aims to understand other cultures from the i nside, by 'standing in their shoes,' 'looking through their eyes' and feeling their pleasure, or pain. Regarding this, Dammak (2015) explains s ocial world should be studied in the natural world through the eyes of the participants, without the intervention of the researcher. It does not follow experimentation, it requires exploration. Interpretivist researchers believe in reality which is based on people's subjective experiment of the external world. There is no single correct route or particular method: knowledge and meaning are acts of interpretation (Kumatongo & Muzata). It is to interpret what the subject is thinking (Kivunja & Kuyini 2017). The theme is that interpretivism b elieves in subjectivity (Taylor & Medina, 2013). Reality and knowledge can be generated by making a close relationship with the participant because it is influenced by anthropology and methodology . The nature of the investigation as mentioned in Taylor and Medina (2013) , inductive with qualitative ethnography, grounded theory approach, narrative in

uyini 2017). The theme is that interpretivism b elieves in subjectivity (Taylor & Medina, 2013). Reality and knowledge can be generated by making a close relationship with the participant because it is influenced by anthropology and methodology . The nature of the investigation as mentioned in Taylor and Medina (2013) , inductive with qualitative ethnography, grounded theory approach, narrative inquiry, phenomenology, and case study are the most commonly found. For instance, ' An Exploration of Bachelor Level Learners' Experiences in Paragraph Writing' c an be a research issue to carry out a study with an interpretive paradigm. To investigate this particular is sue in English education, I can take the interpretive paradigm because I want to explore the learners' views about paragraph writi ng from their perspectives. As Taylor, et al. (2012) discussed this paradigm aims to generate a context-based understanding of people's thoughts, beliefs, values, and associated social norms. I can explore the learners' experiences related to paragraph writing making a very close rapport with them and I can interpret issues from their eyes. Further, I can use a qualitative approach with in - depth interview guidelines (four Bachelor level fourth year English group lear ners from Surkhet Multiple C ampus), and focused group discussion (among six learners) w hich will be an interpretive paradigm alike the features as mentioned in Tubey et al. (2015). The reality that guides this research is pluralism which believes that there is no single reality: not only one way of learning paragraph writing, they experience multiple ways. The learners' multiple perspectives on learning paragraph writing can be 24 | Research Paradigms in English Education: A Brief Overview Surkhet Multiple Campus, Research Management Cell (RMC)   explored by working very closely with them. And knowledge can be interpreted from their worldviews. My aim is neither to generalize the experiences nor emancipate the learning but to understand the situation and how they are learning to write paragraphs. As mentioned in various literature, the positivist seeks to generalize, the critical paradigm seek s to emancipate and the interpretive paradigm seeks to understand the situation with inductive methodology (Scotland, 2012). My lens is to understand how the selected learners are learning to develop a paragraph in

earning but to understand the situation and how they are learning to write paragraphs. As mentioned in various literature, the positivist seeks to generalize, the critical paradigm seek s to emancipate and the interpretive paradigm seeks to understand the situation with inductive methodology (Scotland, 2012). My lens is to understand how the selected learners are learning to develop a paragraph in English. Constructivism Knowledge cannot be generated in a vacuum, it is a social phenomenon which is socially constructed. Interpretivism and constructivism approaches argue that knowledge is produced by exploring and understanding (not discovering) the social wor ld of the people being studied in a particular context (Al- saadi 2014). So, in a sense, i nterpretivism is known as understanding the world, and constructivism is focused on changing the world. The belief is, learning occurs only when learners discover knowledge through the spirit of experimentation and action. It can be illustrated that the ontology of this research is co-constructed: local and specific realism (Guba, 19940), knowledge can be generated from subjective experiences and methodology is inductive , and approaches are as same with interpretivism (Kivunja & Kuyini 2017). For instance, ' Role of Cooperative Learning in Paragraph Writing' can be a research issue to investigate with the world view of constructivism. To solve the problem in this particular issue in English educ ation, I can take the constructivism paradigm because I want to explore the role of cooperative learning in paragraph writing. I will be based on the assumption that working in a team will help to develop writing skills. The knowledge will be constructed by working among the team. The interaction between an individual and one's environment makes one's perception of the world one lives in, and thus reality is socially constructed (Kumatongo & Muzata, 2021). Criticalism The worldview of this paradigm is that knowledge is both socially constructed and influenced by power relativisms from wit hin society, which reflects that historical realism. As mentioned in Scotland (2012), reality has been shaped by social, political, cultural, ethnic, and gender val ues. It is also called the transformative paradigm in Kivunja and Kuyini (2017), which focused on social justice issues and seeks to address the political, social , and econom

ly constructed and influenced by power relativisms from wit hin society, which reflects that historical realism. As mentioned in Scotland (2012), reality has been shaped by social, political, cultural, ethnic, and gender val ues. It is also called the transformative paradigm in Kivunja and Kuyini (2017), which focused on social justice issues and seeks to address the political, social , and economic inequalities in relation to social oppression, conflict, struggle, and power st ructures. So Criticalism is raising questions about traditions and searching for alternatives to solve existing problems. This, t he primary purpose of criticalism is to identify, contest, and help to resolve ' gross power imbalances in society (Taylor & Medina, 2011). A researcher's role is to do advocacy, a change agent, who argues for and leads the way towards a more equitable, fair, and sustainable society. Regarding teaching and learning issues with criticalism, i t is to transfo rm the classroom community through critical action research and support transformative professional practices. Taylor (2014) explains the aim of criticalism is social restructuring. It is making a difference as mentioned in Taylor et al. (2012), for bringing change in the predisposed concept of the oppressed in the society. It can be discussed that the ontology of the critical paradigm is virtual realism (Guba, 1994), shaped by social, political, ethnic, economic, and or gender Dipendra Kumar Khatri | 25@. Surkhet Journal Vol. 2, Issue 1, July 2023, Print ISSN 2362-1230   values, the epistemology is subjective : value- mediated findings and methodology can be quantitative or qualitative. For instance, ' An Exploration of Baadi (an ethnic group in Nepal) Students Experiences in Classroom Learning' can be a research issue to explore with criticalism. To study this particular issue in English education, I can take criticalism paradigm because I want to explore the Baadi learners' experiences in the classroom, especially in English subject class. An in- depth interview can be used as a tool to collect data as critical rese archers have agenda of change and improve the lives and situations of the oppressed (Dammak, 2015). So the aim should be to bring change to the situation and uneasiness faced by Baadi students in their learning environment. Feminism Feminism is related to women's empowerment. From the st

ecially in English subject class. An in- depth interview can be used as a tool to collect data as critical rese archers have agenda of change and improve the lives and situations of the oppressed (Dammak, 2015). So the aim should be to bring change to the situation and uneasiness faced by Baadi students in their learning environment. Feminism Feminism is related to women's empowerment. From the study of a number of literature, it can be explained that feminism is based on the spirit of criticalism to empower the oppressed (Dammak, 2015). Feminism focuses on injustices because of gender as they have historically developed and currently exist in our society. This paradigm aims to improve the situation of women , not only by doing research about them but also by involving them in research for further betterment. It can be illustrated that the ontology o f feminism paradigm research is virtual reali sm (Guba, 1994), shaped by gender values , the epistemology is subjective to seek knowledge about women's experiences, and the methodology is quantitative or qualitative, or both (Ropers-Huilman & Winters, 2011). For instance, ' An Exploration of Female English Head Teachers' Experiences in Managing School Administration' can be a research issue to explore with feminism. To carry out research on this particular issue in English education, I can take the feminism paradigm because I want to explore the female head teacher s' experiences in managing school administration. The objective of this study will not be only to find out the existing experiences of female head teacher s but to enhance their administrative ability . It can be explored quantitatively as well as qualitatively by using surveys and focused group discussions. The focus of feminist theory places gender at the center of its analysis and improves the situation that they faced in their life (Ropers-Huilman & Winters, 2011). So this paradigm looks at the issues from t he gender perspective and gears to emancipate the oppressed ones. Pragmatism The term pragmatism refers to particularit y or context sensitivity. It is situation-specific and assumes that knowledge is constructed differently in a different context with reference to time, place, and situation. Pragmatists are pluralists in that they are inclined to one system of philosophy like positivists and interpretivists. It is termed as multi -strategy or

he oppressed ones. Pragmatism The term pragmatism refers to particularit y or context sensitivity. It is situation-specific and assumes that knowledge is constructed differently in a different context with reference to time, place, and situation. Pragmatists are pluralists in that they are inclined to one system of philosophy like positivists and interpretivists. It is termed as multi -strategy or pragmatic research to generate knowledge from the diverse purpose to solve problems. Although Rahl (2017) states that pragmatism is not affiliated with any system or philosophy, it can be inferred from this statement and other literature that the ontology of this research is pluralism: nonsingular reality (Kivunja & Kuyini, 2017) , and the ep istemology is problem oriented to find the weaknesses and to strengthen it by using mix method approach. As mentioned by Kumatongo and Muzata (2021) the use of mixed design is flexible in terms of 26 | Research Paradigms in English Education: A Brief Overview Surkhet Multiple Campus, Research Management Cell (RMC)   methods, techniques, and procedures that pragmatism implements. For instance, ' Use of ICT in Teaching English during Pandemic in Karnali Province' can be a research iss ue to investigate with the view of pragmatism. To investigate this particular issue in English education, I can take the pragmatism because I want to find out the situation of using ICT in Karnali Province: a particular place, and during the pandemic time: a particular time. As in Kivunja and Kuyini (2017), pragmatism advocates relational epistemology which is appropriate to a particular study and specific time and place. The assumption is that the situation of ICT use may not be the same in all provinces of Nepal. Moreo ver, the use of ICT other than pandemic time, might be different. Post-modernism No human being can see what is going on in another's mind. The truth is not final but ch anging. Many factors play roles in reflecting reality, there are multi-layers in the meaning -making process. Factually, t here is no such window to see what is going on in someone's mind . B ut language and gestures can be the window to look through and draw conclusions making inferences from them. The vision of a rt-based dialogue s, narrative s, and ways of generating knowledge a nd reality is reflected through language, art, and dance. A person what he/she sp

lti-layers in the meaning -making process. Factually, t here is no such window to see what is going on in someone's mind . B ut language and gestures can be the window to look through and draw conclusions making inferences from them. The vision of a rt-based dialogue s, narrative s, and ways of generating knowledge a nd reality is reflected through language, art, and dance. A person what he/she speaks or writes may not be the same in his/her mind. Taylor and Medina (2013) discussed post-modernism under the term 'relatively new paradigm' and explored the concept of 'representation,' the best we can do is represent our thought, and feelings through various means of communication such as language, art, dance, and gestures. Likewise, Taylor (2014) mentioned the 20th century witnessed the unfolding of a post - modern sensibility the linguistic (narrative) turn. It can be discussed that the ontology of this research is pluralism (Taylor et al., 2012), the epistemology is a transformative strategy to address global issues and the methodology is qualitative. For instance, ' The Study of Pictures and Symbols Painted on the Trucks ' can be a research issue to investigate with the post-modernism paradigm. To study this particular issue in English education, I can take the postmodernism paradigm because I want to explore the meaning of pictures and symbols which are painted on the trucks. The painted pictures and symbols on the trucks might give different meanings which can vary what it intends to enta il. As in Taylor and Medina (2013) a person what he/she speaks or writes may not be the same in his/her mind so the meaning may not be the same as shown by t he pictures and symbols on the t rucks. Those might have various meanings than those understood in day-to-day life. Integralism Integralism is the philosophy of bringing together more than one aspect in a single unit. So, integralism is eclectic in nature and integrates multiple paradigms in one research interdependent pluralism (Taylor et al. 2012). Likewise, it is to think holistically about the prospective contribution of multiple research paradigms from post-positivism to post-modernism (Taylor, 2014). It is synonymously used with the term multi -paradigmatic research (Taylor & Medina, 2011). It utilizes some of the new logic of the post -modern paradigms and its central.principle of pluralism: to integrate, to b

lism (Taylor et al. 2012). Likewise, it is to think holistically about the prospective contribution of multiple research paradigms from post-positivism to post-modernism (Taylor, 2014). It is synonymously used with the term multi -paradigmatic research (Taylor & Medina, 2011). It utilizes some of the new logic of the post -modern paradigms and its central.principle of pluralism: to integrate, to bring together, to join, to think, and to embrace (Taylor et al., 2012). It can be illustrated that the ontology of this research paradigm is interdependent paradigm atic pluralism (Taylor et al., 2012), unity in diversity , the Dipendra Kumar Khatri | 27@. Surkhet Journal Vol. 2, Issue 1, July 2023, Print ISSN 2362-1230   epistemology is subjective , and the methodology is mixed. For instance, ' Knowledge and Practices of Novice Researchers in Writing Assignments Regarding the Use of Invention Techniques' can be a research issue to work with the integralism paradigm. To carry out research on this particular issue in English education, I can take integralism paradigm because I want to explore novice researchers ' knowledge and practices about invention techniques in writing assignments. It will be the bringing together of feminism and interpretivism where the use of feminism is purposeful to understand what females know about invention techniques and how they can be enhanced in their writing to develop . It will also integrate an interpretive paradigm to know their feelings about invention techniques and about writing assignments. Taylor and Medina (2013) suggest using a research design that combines two or other paradigms to make multi -paradigmatic research in this 21st century. Conclusion To conclude, the research paradigm is the researcher's looking glass to complete the investigation. As mentioned in Kivunja & Kuyini (2017), the research paradigm is the lens through which a researcher looks at the research world i.e. investigation issue . So, the paradigm is t he mental picture of the object of the research problem. Thus, it is the road map that leads the research purposefully. Research paradigm is the answer to the paradigm fits in which research model. Moreover, it is also based on the holistic aspects of investigation and tools of that particular research. In a nutshell, positivism follows the objective reality in natural science. Moreover, post-positivism

al picture of the object of the research problem. Thus, it is the road map that leads the research purposefully. Research paradigm is the answer to the paradigm fits in which research model. Moreover, it is also based on the holistic aspects of investigation and tools of that particular research. In a nutshell, positivism follows the objective reality in natural science. Moreover, post-positivism further modifies positivism with more interaction and tools to solve the problems in social science. Likewise, Interpretivism and constructivism understand the situation and construct the reality respectively . Similarly, Criticalism and feminism empower the marginalized group and women respectively. Pragmatism intends to investigate and solve contextual problems. Furthermore, postmodernism makes the representative meaning of language, art, and other means of communication and integralism integrates different paradigms within one single research with abductive methodology. In short, the research paradigms are shifting not only with time but also to fulfill the purposes of the particular research issue. Today's researchers are arguing for multiple paradigms as Poni (2014) says the research community has been discussing the dilemmatic issue of mixing research paradigms from positivism to integralism. Postgraduate ABSTRACT: The structure of the research processes is represented according to the paradigmatic nature of the researcher's knowledge and two major differentiation criteria: the DIACHRONIC Structure and the SYNCHRONOUS Structure. Every investigative process varies according to its evo lution over time for the first structure and how it is considered an independent process over time, according to the second structure (Padrón, 1992). Researchers throughout the history of science have developed, shared, and worked under a worldview of real ity that has led them to a system of beliefs around knowledge as ways of accessing and producing the knowledge that they come to be the different paradigms. This belief system has been very divergent from eac h other in different eras of science. Whether or not it coincides is determined by that paradigmatic vision, which in turn explains the techniques, procedures, types of samples, methods, and language used, among other things. KEYWORDS: structure of research processes, epistemological paradigms, nature of knowledge, types of research, research traditions INTRODUCTION Epistemological paradigms of knowledge refer to the fundamental approaches that govern how we understand, acquire and validate knowledge in different fields of knowledge. (Madriz, & Flora, 2012). These paradigms provide a conceptual and methodological framework for addressing questions and problems in research and the philosophy of science. (Soto, 2023) The Positivist Paradigm is based on the idea that knowledge is derived from empirical observation and experimentation. (Bourdeau, 2003) The Hermeneutic Paradigm is focused on the interpretation and understanding of texts and meanings. It has a focus on subjecti vity and deep understanding of human interpretations. It emphasizes the usefulness and applicability of knowledge. It is concerned with how knowledge is applied in practice and solves concrete problems. (Espinosa, Agudelo, & Pachón

(Giraldo, Cruz, & Guarín, 2023) Examines the social and political dimensions of knowledge and seeks emancipation and social transformation. It maintain s that knowledge is constructed by individuals through interaction with their environment. It focus es on subjective perception and personal interpretation. (Morin, (1994) This paradigm is based on the idea that the real world is full of dynamic, interconnected, and adaptive systems that exhibit emergent and nonlinear properties. These epistemological paradigms represent different ways of approaching the search and construction of knowledge. Each has its own philosophical and methodological premises that influence how knowledge is researched, interpreted, and validated in vario us disciplines and contexts. (Taeli Gómez, 2010). The diachronic vision of research defines four phases of these, which are called, respectively: descriptive, explanatory, con trastive and applicative. Which determine typical variations of the investigative processes and that inters ect with the four epistemological paradigms forming sixteen basic patterns for research. The different paradigmatic visions and the different types of research 1. Positivist Paradigm: • Quantitative Research: In this approach, objectivity and precise measurement of phenomena are valued. Quantitative methods are used to collect numerical data and perform statistical analysis. • Experimental: Experimental research focuses on establishing causal relations hips through the manipulation of independent variables and the measurement of effects on dependent variables. • Surveys and Cohort Studies: Surveys and longitudinal studies are used to collect data from large groups of people and analyze trends and correla tions.• Correlational research: is a research approach that aims to explore and measure the relationship or association between two or more variables without directly intervening in them. In other words, the aim is to determine if there is a statistical relationship between the variables, but the aim is not to establish a causal relationship or manipulate any of the variables. • "Ex post facto" or fait accompli research is a research approach that is carried out after the events or phenomena have occur red, that is, it is carried out after the fact and does not involve interventions or direct manipulation of variables. The term "ex post facto" comes from Latin and means "after the fact." This methodology is used to observe and analyze the relationships betwee n variables and events that have already taken place and cannot be controlled or altered by the researcher. However, unlike traditional experimental research, in quasi- experimental research, researchers do not have complete, random control over the assignment of subjects to study groups. However, unlike traditional experimental research, in quasi- experimental research, researchers do not have complete, random control over the assignment of subjects to study groups. Inst ead of randomly assigning participants, groups are often formed based on pre-existing characteristics, such as age, gender, or geographic location. • Historical research is a research process that aims to study and interpret events, people, cultures, and processes that occurred in the past. It is based on the collection and critical analysis of primary and secondary sources, such as documents, letters, d iaries, photographs, records, testimonies, and archaeological evidence, among others. 2. Interpretive Paradigm: • Qualitative Research: Focuses on the deep understanding of human experience and the interpretation of meanings. Qualitative methods are used, such as in-depth interviews, participant observation and content analysis. • Ethnography: Researchers immerse them selves in the culture or context they study to gain a more complete understanding of cultural practices and beliefs. • Case Studies: A case or a group of cases is thoroughly investigated to understand its uniqueness and context. • Grounded Theory research, also known as Grounded Theory in English, is a qualitative research approach that aims to develop theories or conceptual frameworks from data collected in the field. Unlike other approaches that may start with existing theo ries and test predefined hypothe ses, Grounded Theory focuses on generating inductive theories from information obtained during research. • Phenomenological research is a qualitative research approach that focuses on exploring and understanding individuals' subject ive experiences and perc eption of reality. This approach seeks to understand how people experience and make sense of various phenomena, events, or situations in their lives. Phenomenology is based on Husserl's phenomenological philosophy and has been applied in a variety of disciplines, including psychology, sociology, education, and nursing. • Biographical method or life story research is a qualitative research approach that focuses on the study and narration of the life of a person or a group of individuals. This method seeks to understand in depth the lives of the participants

This method seeks to understand in depth the lives of the participants, including their experiences, decisions, changes, and contexts over time. • Symbolic Interactionism research is a sociological and psychological approach that focuses on the study of social interaction and the construction of meanings in everyday life. This approach is based on the idea that people attribute meanings to symbols a nd gestures in their interactions with others, and that these meanings influence their behavior and the way they interpret the w orld around them. 3. Socio-Critical or Constructivist Paradigm: • Participatory Action Research (PAR): Researchers collaborate closely with research participants, and knowledge is constructed through action and reflection. • Artifact-Based Research: Focuses on the creation and analysis of cultural artifacts, such as documents, works of art or technological systems, to understand the social construction of knowledge. • Narrative Research: Personal stories and narratives are used to explore how people construct individual and collective meanings and realities. • Content Analysis research and Discourse Analysis are two qualitative research approaches used to analyze and understand the content of different types of written, oral or visual communication. These appr oaches allow us to systematically and structurally examine the meaning, patterns and trends present in texts, speeches, images or other types of content. • Ethnomethodological research is a sociological approach that focuses on the study of how people create and maintain social order in their everyday interactions. This approach is based on the idea that people use routines and unwritten rules to give meani ng and structure to their social actions and seeks to unravel these underlying rules through detailed observation of social interactions. 4. Complexity or emerging paradigm The complexity paradigm or emergent paradigm is characterized by addressing phenomena that are inherently complex and difficult to reduce to simple components. In this approach, the int erconnection of multiple variables and the influence of contextual factors on the understanding of phenomena are recognized. Research traditions associated with this paradigm include: Complex Systems Research: This research tradition focuses on understandi ng systems that consist of numerous interconnected elements. Researchers analyze how system -level patterns and behaviors emerge from the interactions of its components. Computational models and simulation are common tools in this tradition. Network Theory: Focuses on the study of networks, such as social networks, information networks, or even biological and ecological networks. Researchers explore the structure of connections and how they influence information propagation or system dynamics. Systems Thinking: This tradition focuses on the analysis of complex systems and how changes in one component can have cascading effects throughout the system. It is used to address interdisciplinary problems and to better understand the interrelationships between different parts of a system. Paradigms and Different Types of Research IJSSHR,Volume 06 Issue 12 December 2023 www.ijsshr.in Page 7445 Interconnection: Recognizes that the elements of a complex system are interconnected and that the interactions between them a re fundamental to understanding its behavior. Emergent Properties: Complex systems can exhibit emergent prop erties, meaning that the characteristics of the system cannot be explained simply by analyzing its individual parts. Agent-Based Modeling: Researchers create models that represent individual agents interacting in an environment. This is used to simulate complex phenomena and observe how collective patterns and behaviors emerge from agent interactions. Transdisciplinary Research: Rather than focusing on individual disciplines, transdisciplinary research seeks to address compl ex problems through the collaboration of experts from various disciplines. The goal is to develop comprehensive solutions that

Transdisciplinary Research: Rather than focusing on individual disciplines, transdisciplinary research seeks to address compl ex problems through the collaboration of experts from various disciplines. The goal is to develop comprehensive solutions that consider multiple perspectives and dimensions. Nonlinearity: Relationships within complex systems are often nonlinear, meaning that small changes can have disprop ortionate effects. Holistic approach: Focuses on a holistic understanding rather than a reductionist analysis of systems. Research in Change and Adaptation: In a constantly changing world, this tradition focuses on understanding how organizations, systems and societies adapt and evolve in response to complex and dynamic changes in their environment.In summary, the complexity or emergent paradigm is based on the idea that some phenomena are inherently complex and require research approaches that can capture and understand that complexity. The research traditions mentioned above are examples of how researchers approach problems from this perspective, recognizing the multiple interactions and dimensions involved in complex phenomena.It is important to note that these categories are simplifications and that, in practice, researchers can combine approaches and methods from different paradigms depending on their research objectives and the questions they wish to answer. Furthermore, in recent decades, there has been an increase in the adoption of interdisciplinary approaches that transcend traditional paradigm boundaries, which has enriched research in many areas. Here is a more detailed definition of each of the research traditions of each paradigm: 1. Positivist Paradigm in detail Correlational research is a type of scientific research that focuses on examining and quantifying the relationship between tw o or more variables. This relationship can be positive (when an increase in one variable is related to an increase in the other), negative (when an increase in one variable is related to a decrease in the other) or there is no obvious relationship. Researchers use statistical techniques to analyze data collected from samples or populations and determine the st rength and direction of correlation between variab

iables. This relationship can be positive (when an increase in one variable is related to an increase in the other), negative (when an increase in one variable is related to a decrease in the other) or there is no obvious relationship. Researchers use statistical techniques to analyze data collected from samples or populations and determine the st rength and direction of correlation between variables of interest. Correlational research is valuable for identifying patterns, trends, or statistical relationships in data, but it does not establish causality or allow us to conclude that one variable dire ctly causes changes in the other. In summary, correlational research is useful for exploring and describing statistical relationships between variables, which can provide valuable information to understand associations in natural, social, or psychological phenomena, but it does not allow determining cause and effect between the variables studied. • Experimental research is a scientific research approach in which the researcher deliberately manipulates one or more independ ent variables (factors believed to c ause an effect) to observe their effect on a dependent variable (the outcome being studied). This approach is used to establish cause and effect relationships between variables and is one of the most powerful methodologies for investigating relationships between phenomena. Here is a more detailed definition: Experimental research is a scientific method that focuses on the controlled manipulation of one or more independent variables to examine their impact on a specific dependent variable. The experimental p rocess is carried out in a controlled environment to minimize the influence of confounding or unwanted variables, allowing researchers to attribute observed results to the variab les they are manipulating. Key elements of experimental research include: Manipulation of independent variables: Researchers make deliberate changes to one or more independent variables to see how they affect the dependent variable. Control group: A control group that is not subject to the manipulation of independent variables i s used to compare the results with the experimental group that is. This allows us to evaluate whether the independent variable caused a change in the dependent variable. Randomization: Participants are randomly assigned to the experimental and control grou

o see how they affect the dependent variable. Control group: A control group that is not subject to the manipulation of independent variables i s used to compare the results with the experimental group that is. This allows us to evaluate whether the independent variable caused a change in the dependent variable. Randomization: Participants are randomly assigned to the experimental and control groups to ensure that the groups are comparable and to minimize bias. Control of confounding variables: An effort is made to control and keep constant all variables that are not being manipulated , so that they do not affect the results. Accurate measurement: D ata are collected accurately and systematically to measure the effects of manipulating independent variables on the dependent variable. Paradigms and Different Types of Research IJSSHR,Volume 06 Issue 12 December 2023 www.ijsshr.in Page 7446 Experimental research is especially useful for establishing cause -and-effect relationships because it allows researchers to control and manipulate study conditions. This approach is used in various disciplines, including psychology, biology, physics, and so cial sciences, to answer questions about how variables function and interact in a controlled environment. • Quasi-experimental research is a research approach that shares characteristics with both experimental research and non - experimental (observational) research. In quasi -experimental research, researchers manipulate an independent variable (as in experimental research) but do not have complete control over the assignment of subjects to study groups, which more closely resembles the design of non-experimental research. Here is a more detailed definition: Key features of quasi-experimental research: 1. Manipulation of independent variables: Researchers introduce deliberate changes in one or more independent variables to observe their effect on a specific dependent variable. 2. Comparison groups: Comparison groups are used, but assignment to these groups is not completely ra ndom, but is based on specific criteria or pre-existing characteristics of the subjects. 3. Control of confounding variables: An effort is made to control and hold constant other variables that may influence the re sults, although this may be more difficult to achieve than in traditional experimental research. 4. Lack of complete randomization: The

t assignment to these groups is not completely ra ndom, but is based on specific criteria or pre-existing characteristics of the subjects. 3. Control of confounding variables: An effort is made to control and hold constant other variables that may influence the re sults, although this may be more difficult to achieve than in traditional experimental research. 4. Lack of complete randomization: The assignment of subjects to groups is not done in a complete random manner, which can introduce a certain degree of bias or limit the ability to establish cause and effect relationships with the same confidence as in pure experimental research. Quasi-experimental research is useful when it is unethical or practical to assign participants randomly or when study groups are formed based on pre-existing characteristics. Although it does not offer the same level of control as pure experimental research, it can provide valuable information about the relationships between variables in situations where full experimental manipulation is not feasible. • Historical research is a research approach that focuses on the study and understanding of the past through the critical and systematic examination of sources of historical information. Its main objective is to reconstruct and analyze events, processes, cultures, societies and changes over time. Here is a more detailed definition: Key features of historical research: 1. Gathering Evidence: Historians gather a wide range of sources of historical information to obtain a complete and accurate picture of the past. These sources can be written, visual, oral or material in nature. 2. Critical analysis: A critical and methodological approach is applied to the examination of sources to determine their authenticity, reliability and relevance to the research. 3. Contextualization: Historians seek to understand the context in which historical events and processes occurred, including social, political, cultural and economic factors. 4. Historical Narrative: Historical research involves narrating or constructing a coherent, evidence-based narrative that explains and describes historical events and developments. 5. Interpretation: Historians interpret data and sources to provide a deeper understanding of events and their implications. 6. Historiography: Historians also study historiography, that is, the history of the writing and interpretati

arrative: Historical research involves narrating or constructing a coherent, evidence-based narrative that explains and describes historical events and developments. 5. Interpretation: Historians interpret data and sources to provide a deeper understanding of events and their implications. 6. Historiography: Historians also study historiography, that is, the history of the writing and interpretation of history, to understand how historical perspectives and interpretations have evolved over time. Historical research is fundamental to understanding the evolution of human societies and how past events have shaped the world today. The results of historical research are often presented in the form of books, academic essays, documentaries, and museu m exhibits, among other media, and contribute to our appreciation and understanding of the past and its lessons fo r the present and future. Quantitative research is a scientific research approach that focuses on the collection and analysis of numerical data and sta tistics to understand, describe and explain phenomena, populations or events. This method is based on the measurement and quantification of variables and uses statistical techniques to analyze the data. Here is a more detailed definition: Quantitative research is a scientific research approach that involves the systematic collection of numerical data and its statistical analysis to understand and explain phenomena, populations, or events. In this type of research, variables that can be measure d are identified and numerical data is collected through surveys, questionnaires, structured observations or experiment s. This data is processed and analyzed using statistical techniques with the aim of identifying patterns, relationships and trends. Key features of quantitative research: 1. Measurement of variables: Relevant variables are identified and measured precisely and objectively. These variables can be quantitative (e.g., age, income) or transformed into quantitative data (e.g., opinions rated on a numerical scale). 2. Structured data collection: A standardized and structured methodology is used to collect data, a llowing for comparability and replicability. 3. Statistical analysis: Data are analyzed using statistical methods including descriptive statistics (such as means and stan dard deviations) and inferential analyzes (such as hypothesis testing and re

ve data (e.g., opinions rated on a numerical scale). 2. Structured data collection: A standardized and structured methodology is used to collect data, a llowing for comparability and replicability. 3. Statistical analysis: Data are analyzed using statistical methods including descriptive statistics (such as means and stan dard deviations) and inferential analyzes (such as hypothesis testing and regression analysis) to draw evidence-based conclusions. Generalization: Often, results are sought to be generalized to larger populations or broader contexts, which requires representative samples and appropriate sampling techniques. 5. Objectivity and replicability: Quantitative research strives to be objective and replicable, meaning that other researchers should be able to conduct the same study and obtain similar results using the same data and methods. Quantitative research is especially useful for answering research questions that can be measured and analyzed quantitatively. It is used in a variety of fields, such as psychology, sociology, economics, medicine, and education, to examine relationships, ide ntify patterns, and make data-driven decisions. • Survey research and cohort studies are two research approaches used to collect data in observational studies in the field of social sciences and epidemiology. Definitions of each of these approaches are provided below: • Survey research is a research method that involves the systematic collection of information through standardized questions asked of individuals or study groups. These questions may be open -ended or closed -ended and are administered through written questionnaires, in-person interviews, t elephone, online, or other surveys. The goal of survey research is to obtain quantitative or qualitative data on opinions, attitudes, behaviors, demographic characteristics, and other variables of interest. Surveys are used in a variety of fields, including sociology, psychology, politics, marketing, and public health, among others. • Cohort studies are a specific type of observational research design in which a group of people (the cohort) are followed over time to examine how certain factors or exposures influence their health, behavior, or outcomes over time. weather. These studies a re common in epid

eys are used in a variety of fields, including sociology, psychology, politics, marketing, and public health, among others. • Cohort studies are a specific type of observational research design in which a group of people (the cohort) are followed over time to examine how certain factors or exposures influence their health, behavior, or outcomes over time. weather. These studies a re common in epidemiology and public health. For example, a cohort study might follow a group of people for decades to determine how certain lifestyle factors (such as diet and exercise) influence the incidence of chronic diseases such as diabetes or cancer. Cohort studies can be prospective (starting in the present and following the cohort into the future) or retrospective (using existing data on a cohort that formed in the past). Both approaches, survey research and cohort studies, are valuable for understanding and analyzing a wide range of social and health phenomena. The choice between one or the other depends on the nature of the research questions and whether you seek to collec t data at a single point in time (surveys) or follow a population over a lo nger period (cohort studies). 2. Interpretative Paradigm in detail: • Qualitative research is a scientific research approach used to understand, explore, and analyze in depth the complexity of so cial, human and cultural phenomena. Unlike quantitative resea rch, which focuses on the numerical quantification of data, qualitative research focuses on the collection of descriptive and non-numerical data, such as observations, interviews, documents, and records, to capture the meaning and context of a freak. Here is a more detailed definition: Qualitative research is a scientific research approach that seeks to understand and explore the richness and complexity of so cial, cultural, or human phenomena through the collection and analysis of non-numerical data. In this approach, research techniques such as participant observation, in -depth interviews, content analysis, textual analysis, and document analysis are used to explore the experiences, perspectives, and contexts of the participants or study subjects. Qualitati ve research focuses on the interpretation and understanding of reality from the perspective of the people involved in the phenomenon studied. Key features of qualitative research: Descriptive and context-rich data: Nar

nterviews, content analysis, textual analysis, and document analysis are used to explore the experiences, perspectives, and contexts of the participants or study subjects. Qualitati ve research focuses on the interpretation and understanding of reality from the perspective of the people involved in the phenomenon studied. Key features of qualitative research: Descriptive and context-rich data: Narrative and descriptive data are collec ted that capture the complexity and richness of human and social experiences. In-Depth Exploration: Researchers seek to understand the perspectives and underlying meanings of participants, often using open - ended interviews and immersion in context. Selective sampling: Instead of random samples, purposive or selective sampling is used to choose participants or cases that are especially informative or relevant to the research. Contextualization: Qualitative research focuses on understanding the context in which phenomena occur, including social, cultural, and temporal factors. Methodological flexibility: Researchers can adapt their methods and approaches as they progress through the research, allowing for greater flexibility and responsiveness to emerging findings. Qualitative research is widely used in disciplines such as sociology, psychology, anthropology, education, health, and social sciences in general. It provides a deep understanding of human and social phenomena, and its results can contribute to riche r theories and the formulation of evidence-based policies and practices. • Case study research is a qualitative research approach used to thoroughly explore a particular phenomenon, event, organizatio n, or situation, with the aim of understanding it in its real-world context and providing a detailed and rich description. This methodology involves an exhaustive and in-depth investigation into a specific case or a set of similar cases. Case study research is a qualitative research method that focuses on the close examination of a singular case or a limited set of cases, with the aim of obtaining a deep and contextualized understanding of a phenomenon or situation in its natural setting. This approach involves the collection and analysis of data from multiple s ources, such as interviews, observations, documents, and records, to provide a rich and detailed description of the case or cases studied. Case study research seeks to reveal patterns, relations hips, Paradigms and Different Types of Research IJSSHR,Volume 06 Issue 12 December 2023 www.ijsshr.in Page 7448 contexts, and factors that contribute to a comprehensive understanding of the phenomenon, without attempting to generalize the results to a broader population. This method is used in various disciplines, such as sociology, psychology, education, administration, and social science research, when seeking to deepen the understanding of complex and contextual phenomena. In summary, case study research is a qualitative methodology that focuses on the detailed and contextualized exploration of specific cases to gain an in-depth understanding of a phenomenon or situation in its natural setting. This approach is especially useful when seeking rich, contextualized information rather than broad generalizations. • Ethnographic research is a qualitative research approach used to study and understand the cultural practices, b eliefs, values, and behavior of human groups within their natural and everyday context. This approach involves immersion of the researcher in the environment and culture of the group studied, allowing the researcher to gain a deep and contextualized unders tanding of the experiences and perspectives of the participants. Here is a more detailed definition. Ethnographic research is a qualitative research approach that focuses on the detailed study and understanding of human cultures and communities through par ticipant observation and participation in the study environment. Ethnographic researchers spend time interacting with members of the group studied, participating in their activities, and collecting data through techniques such as direct observation, in-depth interviews, and document review. Key features of ethnographic research: Field Immersion: Researchers spend extended time in the field of study, often living in the community or environment they research, to fully understand the culture and practices of the pa

members of the group studied, participating in their activities, and collecting data through techniques such as direct observation, in-depth interviews, and document review. Key features of ethnographic research: Field Immersion: Researchers spend extended time in the field of study, often living in the community or environment they research, to fully understand the culture and practices of the participants. Participant Observation: Researchers actively participate in the day -to-day activities of the group, allowing them to gain an inside perspective and in-depth understanding of participants' experiences and perspectives. Contextual data collection: Detailed data is collected about cultural practices, social interactions, norms, values, and symbols that are meaningful to the group. Holistic analysis: Data is analyzed holistically, considering the cultural and social context in which it is produced. Rich narrative: The results of ethnographic research are presented in the form of rich, descriptive narratives that show the daily lives and experiences of the participants. Ethnographic research is used in disciplines such as anthropology, sociology, education, psychology, and ethnology to explore and document cultural and social diversity. This approach is especially valuable when seeking to deeply understand the practices and perspectives of human groups within their natural and cultural context. • Grounded Theory research is used in a variety of disciplines, including sociology, psychology, nursing, and education, among others. It is especially useful when seeking to understand complex and poorly understood phenomena and when wanting to develop theories based on empirical data rather than based on pre -existing theories. This approach is valuable for generating knowledge in contexts where existing theory is limited or insufficient. Grounded Theory research is a qualitative method used to develop theories or conceptual models from empirical data collected in the field. This approach involves immersion in the study context and data collection through interviews, observations, or document analysis, as n ecessary. The distinctive characteristic of Grounded Theory is that it does not start from pre -existing theories or hypotheses, but rather allows theories to emerge inductively as data are collected and analyzed. Key features of Grounded Theory research: Open a

d. This approach involves immersion in the study context and data collection through interviews, observations, or document analysis, as n ecessary. The distinctive characteristic of Grounded Theory is that it does not start from pre -existing theories or hypotheses, but rather allows theories to emerge inductively as data are collected and analyzed. Key features of Grounded Theory research: Open and axial coding: The data are analyzed using open coding, where emerging categories and themes are identified, and axial coding, where relationships are established between these categories. Constant comparison: A constant comparison is made between the data collected and the emerging categories, allowing theories to be refined and developed as the research progresses. Theoretical sampling: Selective sampling based on emerging theory is used to obtain additional data to help develop and enric h developing theories. Development of core and subordinate categories: As data is analyzed, core categories are developed that represent key concepts and subordinate categories that explain and detail these concepts. Theory construction: The goal is to build theories or conceptual frameworks that explain the phenomenon studied in a meaningful and profound way. Phenomenological research is a qualitative research approach that focuses on the study of human experiences as they are lived and perceived by participants. Th e goal is to understand and describe in depth how people interpret and give meaning to their experiences, as well as to identify underlying patterns and structures in their stories. Key features of phenomenological research: Focus on subjective experience: It focuses on the subjective experiences and perceptions of individuals, trying to capture what people feel and how they interpret their reality. Phenomenological reduction: Researchers apply a technique called "phenomenological reduction" to suspend prio r judgments and preconceptions and focus on the experience as it is lived by the participants. s lived by the participants. In-depth interviews: Data collection is carried out through in-depth interviews in which participants describe their experiences and the researcher seeks to understand and clarify these descriptions. Thematic analysis: The data is systematically analyzed, and recurring themes and p atterns are identified in the participants' narratives.
