According to Morgan (2007), pragmatism addresses three issues with the metaphysical para- digm: ‘‘how to define paradigms, whether those paradigms were incommensurate, and the extent to which metaphysical assumptions actually guide research in the social sciences’’ (p. 66). He argues that rather than relying on the metaphysical paradigm’s a priori ‘‘limits on communication,’’ pragmatism emphasizes creating ‘‘shared meanings and joint action’’ (p. 67). This emphasis points to the underlying belief in complementarity, that is, qualitative and quan- titative approaches can be combined in order to ‘‘compliment’’ the advantages and disadvan- tages present within each. The impact of this complementarity approach can be clearly seen in Arnon & Reichel’s (2009) discussion of their study using a telephone survey to comparatively analyze perceptions of a ‘‘good teacher’’ across gender and ethnicity among Israeli citizens. They used a concurrent parallel design with a telephone interview that included open-ended qualitative questions and a quantitative survey. These data were then analyzed separately and integrated using two meth- ods: ‘‘cross-validation’’ or triangulation and ‘‘quantizing’’ the qualitative codes for statistical analysis (p. 182). Methodologically, the discussion of their resear

ng Israeli citizens. They used a concurrent parallel design with a telephone interview that included open-ended qualitative questions and a quantitative survey. These data were then analyzed separately and integrated using two meth- ods: ‘‘cross-validation’’ or triangulation and ‘‘quantizing’’ the qualitative codes for statistical analysis (p. 182). Methodologically, the discussion of their research centers on the importance of having this pragmatic, complementary approach to their data since their quantitative and qualitative data seemed inconsistent with one another. In other words, despite having disso- nance at the integration stage, Arnon and Reichel (2009) were able to use their pragmatic per- spective to still emphasize the ‘‘shared meanings’’ created as a result of the integration. This reflects the intersubjectivity that pragmatism offers mixed methods researchers, which empha- sizes disrupting the dichotomy between ‘‘complete objectivity’’ and ‘‘complete subjectivity’’ (Morgan, 2007, p. 71). Similarly, the researchers stated that this complementarity was possible in their own working relationships because they ‘‘could speak a common research language and combine [their] diverse research experience and expertise in a joint project’’ (Arnon & Reichel, 2009, p. 178). Sharp et al. (2012) also discussed the impact of this complementarity in the formation a research team from various disciplines. In their article analyzing the impact of pragmatism on their sam- pling in a multisite case study, Sharp et al. (2012) argued that having such diversity was neces- sary in order to engage in a ‘‘mixed methods way of thinking’’ (Greene, 2007). The pragmatic approach also maintains the ‘‘valuable contributions’’ of the metaphysical paradigm, that is, the importance of epistemology and the centrality of one’s world views for their research (Morgan, 2007). While considering these issues, pragmatism focuses on what things will make a difference, as well as connecting abstract issues on the epistemological level to the methodological level. Arnon and Reichel (2009), for example, sought to engage in con- ceptions of multiculturalism and how identities can affect beliefs. It was toward this purpose that the researchers ‘‘gave priority’’ in their research design ‘‘according to [their] pragmatist approach’’ (p. 178). At this conceptual stage, Arnon and Reichel explicitly

t issues on the epistemological level to the methodological level. Arnon and Reichel (2009), for example, sought to engage in con- ceptions of multiculturalism and how identities can affect beliefs. It was toward this purpose that the researchers ‘‘gave priority’’ in their research design ‘‘according to [their] pragmatist approach’’ (p. 178). At this conceptual stage, Arnon and Reichel explicitly state that pragma- tism influenced their conceptualizations of ‘‘the nature of the investigated phenomena, and the [writing of] research questions and then considered the appropriate methodological ways of inquiry’’ (p. 178). Finally, pragmatism offers several ways to bridge dichotomies that exist in mixed methods approaches to social science. Biesta (2010) addresses this by arguing that ‘‘knowledge’’ can only provide us information about our actions and their results, not of the ‘‘once-and-for-all truths’’ (p. 96). Instead, pragmatism breaks down the hierarchies between positivist and con- structivist ways of knowing in order to look at what is meaningful from both (Biesta, 2010). Addressing the connections between theory and data, pragmatism uses ‘‘abduction,’’ which ‘‘moves back and forth between induction and deduction—first converting observations into Shannon-Baker 325 theories and then assessing those theories through action’’ (Morgan, 2007, p. 71). An example of a research study that explicitly uses pragmatism’s abduction is by Feilzer (2010). In this arti- cle, she discusses the implications pragmatism had on her research study measuring the impact on the general public of receiving factual information concerning crime via newspapers. According to Feilzer, she ‘‘abductively’’ noticed while analyzing the qualitative data that it ‘‘had qualities of stability and recurrence,’’ which prompted her to transform it into quantitative data (p. 10). She characterized her use of abduction as ‘‘moving back and forth between the data sets,’’ combining it with methods for triangulation (p. 12). Also, pragmatism utilizes transferability to consider the implications of research. Transferability in qualitative research refers to the possible local and external connections that data can reveal about a phenomenon (Jensen, 2008). In other words, transferability allows researchers to ‘‘investigate the factors that affect whether the knowledge we gain can be trans- ferred to oth

ulation (p. 12). Also, pragmatism utilizes transferability to consider the implications of research. Transferability in qualitative research refers to the possible local and external connections that data can reveal about a phenomenon (Jensen, 2008). In other words, transferability allows researchers to ‘‘investigate the factors that affect whether the knowledge we gain can be trans- ferred to other settings’’ (Morgan, 2007, p. 72). Returning to the example offered by Arnon and Reichel (2009), the authors conclude by pointing out the implications of their methods with feedback for researchers intending to use a similar ‘‘multistrand-conversion-concurrent design,’’ a telephone survey, and/or using a survey instrument with both quantitatively and qua- litatively driven questions (p. 191). They also ask pertinent questions on the implications of their research findings for investigating these culturally laden beliefs about a ‘‘good teacher’’ in other national contexts. Transformative-Emancipation2 Mertens (2003) makes an argument against using a pragmatic approach simply because it is practically based. She raises the question ‘‘practical for whom and to what end?’’ (House & Howe, 1999, as cited in Mertens, 2003, p. 159). In response to this, Mertens (2010) argues for adopting particular goals for research, which can be done by utilizing the transformative- emancipatory perspective. This perspective is characterized by the intentional collaboration with minority and marginalized groups or those whose voice is not typically heard on particular issues. According to Mertens (2010), it has been used in disabilities and deafness communities, work on HIV/AIDS, violence prevention, participation in governing structures, and more. This perspective necessitates attention to power, privilege, and voice throughout the research process (Mertens, 2003). It emphasizes making explicit the researcher’s values, ethics, and paradigm(s) (Creswell & Plano Clark, 2011; Mertens, 2010). This perspective was developed in concert with the increase in national awareness on issues related to race, class, gender, and disabilities. It emphasizes considering who is being left out, what groups are being targeted, and how researchers label/recruit/treat. Chilisa (2005), for example, analyzed research in Botswana on the prevention of HIV/AIDS to uncover how these studies have marginalized Afri

perspective was developed in concert with the increase in national awareness on issues related to race, class, gender, and disabilities. It emphasizes considering who is being left out, what groups are being targeted, and how researchers label/recruit/treat. Chilisa (2005), for example, analyzed research in Botswana on the prevention of HIV/AIDS to uncover how these studies have marginalized African value systems. I would argue that these issues of power inequities and voice should be paid particular attention in studies that rely on a triangulation rationale for the use of mixed methods. For example, how can inferences from qualitative and quantitative data be triangulated if collected from samples at the top and bottom of a bureau- cracy? Conversely, how might an imbalance in power among sample populations influence using a mixed methods approach to discover ‘‘initiation,’’ or differences or paradoxes (Greene, Caracelli, & Graham, 1989)? Thus, integrating the data or results from two different samples under this perspective could be problematic unless the researcher’s goal is to highlight the divergences that exist between the two groups. Mertens (2003) argues that while an in-depth literature review is an important first step in determining the boundaries of what has been published, this approach to information collecting is limited to only that which has been published. Thus, Mertens argues for building strong 326 Journal of Mixed Methods Research 10(4) relationships within the community. To utilize a transformative-emancipatory perspective, the researcher must have a strong understanding of the community, its history (particularly related to research), and be well connected if not integrated within the community. Mixed methods researchers might plan for this kind of relationship building as part of their qualitative stage in a sequential exploratory (Creswell & Plano Clark, 2011) design for example. It is important to keep in mind though that for some communities, the use of certain methods that have histori- cally negatively affected the community might cause them to be hesitant or even resistant to the research. This emphasis on being integrated within the community is evident throughout the ‘‘check- list’’ provided by Canales (2013) for transformative mixed methods research done with Mexican Americans. This checklist was developed as a frame for oth

ies, the use of certain methods that have histori- cally negatively affected the community might cause them to be hesitant or even resistant to the research. This emphasis on being integrated within the community is evident throughout the ‘‘check- list’’ provided by Canales (2013) for transformative mixed methods research done with Mexican Americans. This checklist was developed as a frame for other researchers to use in order to ensure that their research embodied high-quality mixed methods, critical and feminist theories, and a transformative-emancipatory perspective. Related to Mertens’s (2007) emphasis on getting information not just from the literature, Canales (2013) lists the following: ‘‘learn about the Mexican American community and its perceptions of the issue or problem under study from Mexican Americans themselves’’ (p. 10, italics added). She also lists that mixed methods researchers develop ‘‘trust’’ and ‘‘rapport’’ with the community (p. 10). Beyond building relationships with communities and viewing them as research ‘‘participants,’’Mertens (2003) states that community people should be actually involved in the research. This can be through question development, determining culturally appropriate meth- ods, effective recruitment, sensitive data collection, and so on. Since the transformative- emancipatory perspective advocates the use of marginalized voices within the research, mixed methods researchers can operationalize this in the use of their qualitative results. For example, a sequential explanatory design could be used to highlight the voices of the participants in terms of how they understand the research phenomenon from their own perspective. This type of design utilizes qualitative data collection after the quantitative strand in order to explain or further understand the quantitative data, among other purposes (Creswell & Plano Clark, 2011). This emphasis on community involvement is evident in the checklist from Canales (2013), though not explicitly. For example, when Canales described how to relate the ‘‘benefits and credibility’’ of a mixed methods design to the transformative-emancipatory perspective, she argues that researchers should hire members of the community to collect data. I speculate that empowering the involvement of the local community in something like data collection could assist mixed methods researchers utilizing a

plicitly. For example, when Canales described how to relate the ‘‘benefits and credibility’’ of a mixed methods design to the transformative-emancipatory perspective, she argues that researchers should hire members of the community to collect data. I speculate that empowering the involvement of the local community in something like data collection could assist mixed methods researchers utilizing a concurrent parallel design that requires collection of qualitative and quantitative data simultaneously. According to Canales, this involvement ‘‘benefits the community by providing pay and research training to its members and promotes credibility through collaboration with the community’’ (p. 16). Thus, involving the community in the analytic and integration stages of a mixed methods study could further benefit the com- munity beyond simply the products of the research. Of particular issue to the transformative-emancipatory perspective during data collection is the researcher’s ability to explicate how the research will benefit the community, whether the community will view the findings as credible, the appropriateness of the communication, use of culturally sensitive ways to collect data, and ensuring data are used to positively change the community (Mertens, 2007). Canales’s (2013) checklist in particular addresses issues of cul- tural sensitivity, asking how the research questions are posed, whether the measures used are ‘‘culturally equivalent,’’ and if the research will ‘‘validate the culture of Mexican American female participants’’ (p. 11). In terms of data analysis, reporting, and using results, Mertens (2003) stresses that within this perspective it is important to connect quantitative and qualitative data in analysis, look at subgroups to determine any impacts across diversities, interpret results through a lens focused Shannon-Baker 327 on power dynamics, and ensure results are reported in ways that promote social change. By way of advocating for investigating power dynamics, the transformative-emancipatory perspec- tive provides mixed methods researchers with a potential lens from which to base their integra- tion analysis for example. I would argue also that data transparency, considered a characteristic of quality mixed methods research (O’Cathain, 2010), after the research study can then assist in maintaining a positive relationship with the communi

power dynamics, the transformative-emancipatory perspec- tive provides mixed methods researchers with a potential lens from which to base their integra- tion analysis for example. I would argue also that data transparency, considered a characteristic of quality mixed methods research (O’Cathain, 2010), after the research study can then assist in maintaining a positive relationship with the community. Overall, within the transformative-emancipatory perspective, it is important to frame all deci- sions, data collection, analysis, and reporting within the social and historical contexts of the community. This means paying particular attention to issues of power, privilege, and voice. As detailed above, this perspective offers mixed methods overarching goals to help guide the entire research process, particular considerations for the use of the qualitative data and results depend- ing on design, and suggestions for the focus of integration analyses. Dialectics Dialectics offers a different approach to the paradigm discussion. Instead of conceptualizing another ‘‘paradigm’’ or perspective entirely, dialectics argues for using two or more paradigms together. According to Greene and Hall (2010), a dialectic perspective brings together two or more paradigms in ‘‘respectful dialogue’’ with one another throughout the research process (p. 124). By doing this, dialectics values each paradigm as a unique perspective of the world (Greene, 2007). The emphasis here is not in actually joining the two, but instead focusing on the tensions and new understandings that arise. Dialectics is used as a perspective to address divergent or dissonant data (Mathison, 1988) uncovered when bringing together paradigms with conflicting ideas (Greene & Caracelli, 1997). Among the perspectives discussed here, dialectics offers the most emphasis on divergence in the data and results. This focus on divergence is reminiscent of Greene’s (2007)‘‘initiation’’ rationale for mixed methods research. In the field of evaluation studies, Johnson and Stefurak (2013) take on the question of what evidence is considered credible from a ‘‘dialectical pluralism’’ perspective. Emphasizing the process-based component of dialectics, Johnson and Stefurak use this perspective to bring together and support cross-perspective research teams. Like the transformative-emancipatory perspective, dialectics also recognizes

ield of evaluation studies, Johnson and Stefurak (2013) take on the question of what evidence is considered credible from a ‘‘dialectical pluralism’’ perspective. Emphasizing the process-based component of dialectics, Johnson and Stefurak use this perspective to bring together and support cross-perspective research teams. Like the transformative-emancipatory perspective, dialectics also recognizes the importance of researcher’s values on the research process (Johnson & Stefurak, 2013). It is through this recognition that opposing values are equally important and have something offer that dialectics can bring diverse researcher teams and paradigmatic perspectives together. Johnson and Stefurak (2013) also argue for the collection of evidence to support changes in both top-down and bottom-up models. In other words, not only can dialectics provide a frame- work for bringing together potentially opposing perspectives and data, but it also supports social change. Thus, dialectics offers a framework regarding the handling and analysis of data, relationships among researchers and with participants, and dialogue across paradigmatic per- spectives. It stresses no binaries, equal weight between groups, and no hierarchies (Johnson & Stefurak, 2013), making this an ideal paradigm to consider when placing equal priority among strands in a mixed methods study. Frels, Frels, and Onwuegbuzie (2011) recently argued for the increased use of geographic information systems (GIS) to address the seldom investigated spatial contexts relevant to busi- ness and management. In discussing the possibilities of GIS for these fields, Frels et al. identify ‘‘dialectical pluralism’’ as having the ‘‘greatest potential to use GIS applications to their fullest extent’’ (p. 371). This is due to the perspective’s ability to ‘‘incorporate’’ differing epistemolo- gies present in typical uses of GIS compared to business and management research. 328 Journal of Mixed Methods Research 10(4) Greene and Hall (2010) also emphasize maintaining a reflective stance throughout the inquiry in order to ensure that any inferences made are ‘‘warranted’’ and thus not a result of the researcher’s biases (p. 138). These reflections are also important in order to generate deep, rich, and complex insights from the data. Ultimately, dialectics seeks to ‘‘surface, engage and legiti- mize difference in the social world

2010) also emphasize maintaining a reflective stance throughout the inquiry in order to ensure that any inferences made are ‘‘warranted’’ and thus not a result of the researcher’s biases (p. 138). These reflections are also important in order to generate deep, rich, and complex insights from the data. Ultimately, dialectics seeks to ‘‘surface, engage and legiti- mize difference in the social world, toward greater understanding and acceptance of differ- ence’’ (p. 140). This emphasis on reflection makes dialectics useful for mixed methods designs based in grounded theory and keeping research memos in general (Wiener, 2007). In terms of the research process, the dialectic perspective believes that the methods used should depend on the study at hand. The researcher(s) should collect, analyze, and report data in ways that promotes dialogue, particularly between the quantitative and qualitative data sets (Greene & Hall, 2010). For example, mixed methods researchers can use side-by-side compari- sons or comparative data displays that bring together both the qualitative and quantitative strands (Creswell & Plano Clark, 2011). This incites inferences based on these cross-dialogues including addressing those points that raise tensions, contradictions, and paradoxes. As a result, dialectics offers mixed methods research a perspective that not only recognizes difference and diversity but also emphasizes its importance to the point of placing it at the center of all deci- sions and outputs. This perspective is also a mechanism for mixed methods researchers to com- bine two or more paradigms that might be of relevance to their project (Greene & Hall, 2010). Critical Realism While some disciplines have more readily accepted the complementarity argument for para- digms, in other disciplines the paradigm wars have resulted in further ‘‘fragmentation and polarization,’’ impeding the adoption of perspectives like dialectics (Modell, 2009, p. 208). In response to this polarization, some researchers use a critical realist perspective. Discussed as a ‘‘stance’’ (Maxwell & Mittapalli, 2010), critical realism 3 utilizes the compatibility thesis of worldviews, supporting the point that quantitative and qualitative research can work together to address the other’s limitations. Embodying a constructivist epistemology, critical realism believes in a world that is constructed through o

me researchers use a critical realist perspective. Discussed as a ‘‘stance’’ (Maxwell & Mittapalli, 2010), critical realism 3 utilizes the compatibility thesis of worldviews, supporting the point that quantitative and qualitative research can work together to address the other’s limitations. Embodying a constructivist epistemology, critical realism believes in a world that is constructed through our individual standpoints and perceptions (Creswell & Plano Clark, 2011). However, this is contextualized within the traditional realist ontology where reality can exist outside of perception (Maxwell & Mittapalli, 2010). Thus, while there may be multiple perspectives on a single event or object (Healy & Perry, 2000), critical realism also recognizes that there are realities that cannot not be known (Guba, 1990). As a result, critical realists believe that theories can only be impartial representations of reality. Still, the goal of critical realist research is to measure and verify underlying structures in reality (Bisman, 2010). Similarly, critical realists also believe that objectivity can only be approxi- mated (Maxwell & Mittapalli, 2010). The implications of critical realism’s ontology are represented throughout the practical use of this perspective. For example, due to this belief, critical realists use theory as helpful in guid- ing the research process, while recognizing that such theories are impartial or otherwise incom- plete views of reality. I believe this manifests in mixed methods research based on the ability to discuss how particular findings might not support existing theories. This is not to underestimate the influence of theory on research but to recognize instead that theories cannot offer an all- encompassing or complete view of a phenomenon. Identified as ‘‘compatible’’ with both quantitative and qualitative research, critical realism emphasizes a particular view of causal relationships. Like dialectics, critical realism emphasizes process and a ‘‘generative’’ view (Clark, MacIntyre, & Cruickshank, 2007) of coming to infer- ences during the integration stage. By emphasizing the process, critical realism offers a recogni- tion of relationships between factors that promotes the investigation of context-based causality. nerative’’ view (Clark, MacIntyre, & Cruickshank, 2007) of coming to infer- ences during the integration stage. By emphasizing the process, critical realism offers a recogni- tion of relationships between factors that promotes the investigation of context-based causality. Shannon-Baker 329 It does this by seeking to ‘‘understand how phenomena are generated—in either physical or social realms—[. . . examining] underlying factors that are or potentially are at play’’ (Clark et al., 2007, p. 524). Therefore, critical realists are interested in ‘‘particular situations and events, rather than addressing only general patterns’’ (Maxwell & Mittapalli, 2010, p. 156). For example, Clark et al. (2007) use a ‘‘critical realist approach’’ in order to critique other research on programs designed to help ‘‘reduce cardiovascular risks and promote health in people with heart disease’’ (p. 513). They argue that contemporary research is based on an ‘‘impoverished ontology’’ related to positivism that does not take into account the contextual factors that influ- ence the health and outcomes of such programs (Clark et al., 2007, p. 513). In another example of mixed methods research utilizing critical realism’s emphasis on con- texts, Douglas, Gray, and van Teijlingen (2010) used a realist approach in their study evaluating smoking cessation intervention programs. They discuss using this perspective specifically in order to illuminate ‘‘thecontext in which an intervention is implemented; themechanisms of that intervention, as well as its outcomes’’ (p. 2). For this study, in particular, it was important to identify the contexts of successful intervention programs since these contextual factors are intimately connected to the success or failure of an intervention. To address this importance, they used a phased design that first mapped the ‘‘context, components and complexity of emer- gent health promotion interventions’’ (p. 3). These contextual maps could then be used to ground their inferences. Critical realism shares with pragmatism the utilization of abduction when applying theory to the data. For example, Modell (2009) discusses two articles that use a critical realist–like approach to triangulation. (Modell states that they are not arguing that the articles use critical realism.) Both examples used an abduction model to bring together multiple theories in order to ‘‘explain

ferences. Critical realism shares with pragmatism the utilization of abduction when applying theory to the data. For example, Modell (2009) discusses two articles that use a critical realist–like approach to triangulation. (Modell states that they are not arguing that the articles use critical realism.) Both examples used an abduction model to bring together multiple theories in order to ‘‘explain context-specific tendencies’’ (p. 218). This approach was used because, according to Modell, the researchers found that traditional approaches to the data could not explain the find- ings that seemed to contradict existing theories. In this way, critical realism offered an approach to add new ‘‘theoretical insights’’ while still attending to particular contexts vis-a`-vis abduction. While both perspectives use abduction, critical realism in particular emphasizes the theory- generating and generalizability of results as well as their context-based nature. Furthermore, this allows for the continual refinement of theories (Modell, 2009). Critical realism also emphasizes the ‘‘mental,’’‘‘part of reality,’’ or how emotion and mental perspectives are important to the research process (Modell, 2009). Critical realists place high importance on perspectives—that is, taking new perspectives, understanding different view- points, and representing diverse voices. Modell (2009) would argue that these diverse view- points can be seen as ‘‘the starting point for further theorizing’’ (p. 219). In fact, ignoring variations in perspectives would make critical realists, according to Modell, ‘‘highly skeptical’’ since it would likely compromise the overall analysis (p. 219). Critical realism can offer mixed methods a perspective that emphasizes diversity and the relationships among people, events, and ideas. This allows for process-based causal inferences, which are not as possible with other perspectives described here. Finally, critical realism also offers mixed methods researchers a perspective that emphasizes perspective-taking and empow- ering the voices of others while still recognizing that these can only be partial representations of reality (Maxwell & Mittapalli, 2010). These components combined make this paradigmatic perspective particularly useful in evaluation-based research studies. Conclusions In this article, I have discussed the history and various conceptualizations o

ctive that emphasizes perspective-taking and empow- ering the voices of others while still recognizing that these can only be partial representations of reality (Maxwell & Mittapalli, 2010). These components combined make this paradigmatic perspective particularly useful in evaluation-based research studies. Conclusions In this article, I have discussed the history and various conceptualizations of paradigm and the ‘‘paradigm issue’’ for mixed methods research, its importance to mixed methods, and four of 330 Journal of Mixed Methods Research 10(4) the ‘‘perspectives’’ or philosophical foundations that have particular implications for mixed methods inquiry. By focusing on how these perspectives—that is, pragmatism, transformative- emancipation, dialectics, and critical realism—influence mixed methods design, theory use, relationships, inferences, and data reporting, this discussion is intended to be practically based for novice mixed methods researchers as well as relevant to those interested in the paradigm issue. Pragmatism offers a strong emphasis on research questions, communication, and shared meaning making. In connecting theory to data, it uses abduction, which has been found to be particularly useful during the integration stage of mixed methods. Pragmatism recommends a balance between subjectivity and objectivity throughout the inquiry. Finally, its emphasis on transferability offers a paradigm that can revise previous or create new disciplinary theories based in particular context but still generalizable to others. The transformative-emancipatory perspective specifically addresses social inequities in order to enact positive social change related to oppression, power, and privilege. In working with marginalized groups, voice and power are particularly important considerations that should be addressed in all stages of the mixed methods design. Theoretical frameworks, methods, and the researcher all must have strong relationships to the communities involved. Emphasizing values, this perspective offers mixed methods inquiry specific value-based goals to be incorporated at all stages. Taking a somewhat different stance compared to the other perspectives, dialectics offers a way to bridge seemingly conflicting ideas, theories, and/or data sets. In particular, this perspec- tive is interested in investigating the nature of these divergences as well as po

Emphasizing values, this perspective offers mixed methods inquiry specific value-based goals to be incorporated at all stages. Taking a somewhat different stance compared to the other perspectives, dialectics offers a way to bridge seemingly conflicting ideas, theories, and/or data sets. In particular, this perspec- tive is interested in investigating the nature of these divergences as well as possible conver- gences. As a result, it can be used as a perspective to help address divergences between data sets after integration. Promoting reflection and dialogue throughout, dialectics also offers diverse research teams a frame within which to work together. Finally, critical realism also bridges divides between quantitative and qualitative approaches but is based specifically in the belief that theories on reality are partial thus emphasizing the importance of diverse viewpoints. This ontology then allows for researchers to legitimately dis- cuss divergences in their findings compared to existing literature. Critical realism can help facil- itate dialogue across differences theoretically. It also encourages including insights that are mentally based, such as collecting perception- and reflection-based data. Its emphasis on rela- tionships is connected to its ability to infer causal relationships that are both contextually based and generalizable to others. This perspective has been particularly used in evaluation studies. Through discussion of examples of recent mixed methods research that explicitly engages each of these perspectives, this article offers insights into particular considerations, mechan- isms, and avenues in which researchers can mobilize these perspectives intentionally in their research. While this discussion is not intended to be exhaustive, it does provide a foundation for considering which perspectives might be relevant to a novice researcher based on her or his beliefs and research focus. In most cases, the strategies for intentionally using these perspec- tives were those addressed in the examples discussed above. In a few cases, I used a side-by-side analysis of the articles with the researchers’ identified paradigmatic perspective to uncover any other influences. Future researchers could use the same approach by asking how a particular decision made during the research inquiry aligns with a specific component of her or his paradigm. Ad

e perspec- tives were those addressed in the examples discussed above. In a few cases, I used a side-by-side analysis of the articles with the researchers’ identified paradigmatic perspective to uncover any other influences. Future researchers could use the same approach by asking how a particular decision made during the research inquiry aligns with a specific component of her or his paradigm. Additionally, reading further examples of empirical research that highlight the use of paradigm(s) will also generate ideas for the researcher’s own study. While examples of this reporting do exist and journals are increasingly asking for such discussions (e.g.,Journal of Mixed Methods Research), these examples are still few and far between. Additionally, while several articles were found that identified using dialectics to help reconcile competing beliefs, Shannon-Baker 331 much of these did not adequately discuss how this was done. It is necessary then that our dis- cussions on paradigms promote such intentional uses of these perspectives. While in this discussion, I did intentionally use the termperspective, I still argue for the importance of conducting paradigmatically grounded mixed methods research. There are strong arguments on how the term or concept ofparadigm has become unhelpful at best. It remains to be decided in the field whether replacingparadigm with another term would be useful or if such debate is pulling discussions away from other important issues. However, we should not argue for a single ‘‘best’’ paradigm (or perspective) for mixed methods research. To do so would perhaps enliven a new kind of paradigm war, placing impor- tance on research that uses said paradigm over others when this kind of exclusionary thinking helped prompt the paradigm war as we have come to know it. Thus, the particular paradigm(s) used should be at the discretion of the researcher(s). We should be less concerned with whether researchers chose the ‘‘best’’ paradigm and more concerned with their legitimation for and operationalization of the one chosen. Does it align with her or his values and the research focus? Are the implications of the paradigm clearly and explicitly discussed? How do her or his infer- ences relate to the discussed paradigm(s)? We should be focused more on how researchers use their paradigm(s) and their elaborations on how. To do so better promotes res

ned with their legitimation for and operationalization of the one chosen. Does it align with her or his values and the research focus? Are the implications of the paradigm clearly and explicitly discussed? How do her or his infer- ences relate to the discussed paradigm(s)? We should be focused more on how researchers use their paradigm(s) and their elaborations on how. To do so better promotes researchers’ explicit and intentional interaction with their philosophical foundations throughout the design, imple- mentation, and reporting stages of their research. In this article, I refer to philosophical paradigms and theoretical frameworks as two distinct entities. A philosophical paradigm as a worldview with certain beliefs about how knowledge is constructed and measureable, what is ethical, and the nature of being. A theoretical framework, on the other hand, serves as an explanation for a phenomenon, a general ‘‘orienting lens’’ for understanding a phenom- enon (Creswell, 2009, p. 62), and provides a hypothesis for what might occur or why. 2. ================================================================================ HOPSCOTCH STEP 1: Step 1: Who am I as a researcher ================================================================================ BROADER CONCEPTS AND TERMS ---------------------------------------- - Worldview/Paradigm/Paradigmatic view (Knowledge)/Paradigmatic positioning/Research Identity SPECIFIC RESEARCH TERMS AND SUB-TOPICS ---------------------------------------- - Positivism - Post-Positivism - Constructivism - Pragmatism - Transformative DEFINITIONS AND KEY CONCEPTS ---------------------------------------- Worldview, paradigm, or paradigmatic positioning (definitions & main ones) -A Worldview is a basic set of beliefs that guide action (Guba, 1990, p. 17) -Creswell (2007) defines a worldview as a fundamental set of beliefs or assumptions that guide how a researcher approaches and conducts their inquiry. These beliefs shape the researcher's understanding of the world, influencing their research design, data collection methods, and interpretation of findings. -Positivist worldview: A paradigm that views knowledge as objective and measurable, emphasizing empirical observation, quantifiable data, and the scientific method to uncover universal laws. -Post-Positivist worldview: An evolution of positivism that recognizes the limitations of absolute objectivity. It values empirical inquiry but acknowledges that all observations are theory-laden and subject to potential bias. -Constructivist worldview: A worldview that sees knowledge as socially constructed and subjective. It emphasizes understanding individuals’ experiences and meanings through qualitative methods and contextual interpretation -Pragmatic worldview: A practical and flexible paradigm that focuses on research questions and real-world outcomes, often combining qualitative and quantitative methods to find workable solutions. -Transformative worldview: A paradigm rooted in social justice and advocacy. It aims to challenge inequality and empower marginalized groups, often using participatory and critical research approaches. ================================================================================ HOPSCOTCH STEP 2: Step 2: What am I wondering about? ================================================================================ BROADER CONCEPTS AND TERMS ---------------------------------------- - Research Topic/Area of Study - Research Goals SPECIFIC RESEARCH TERMS AND SUB-TOPICS ---------------------------------------- - Statement of: Elements under investigation, Concepts under study - Personal goals/Practical goals/Intellectual goals DEFINITIONS AND KEY CONCEPTS ---------------------------------------- Research Topic: A research topic is the broad, general area of interest or inquiry that a study seeks to explore. It represents the initial starting point of the research process—a thematic umbrella under which more specific problems, questions, and objectives are later defined. It answers the basic question: “What is this research about?” Research Goals: Maxwell (2008) states that goals include motives, desires, and purposes—anything that leads you to do the study or that you hope to accomplish by doing it. Problem Statement: The problem statement identifies a specific issue, gap, or concern that needs investigation. It clearly articulates what’s wrong, what’s missing, or what’s misunderstood in the current state of knowledge, practice, or theory. In essence, it’s the why this study needs to exist. A good problem statement: Defines the context and background of the proposed study: Describes what the topic and/or problem under study is; Indicates the significance or consequences of not addressing it, and; Points toward a knowledge or practice gap. Purpose Statement: The purpose statement flows directly from the problem statement and articulates the intent of the study. It lays out what the researcher plans to accomplish—clearly, concisely, and with direction. It typically answers: What is the study trying to achieve? What is being studied (variables, population, setting), and; What is the methodological approach (qualitative, quantitative, mixed)? Research question: A research question is the foundational, guiding inquiry that drives a research study. It articulates exactly what the researcher seeks to understand, explain, predict, or explore within the scope of a specific problem and purpose. It usually emerges after the problem has been identified and the purpose has

What is the methodological approach (qualitative, quantitative, mixed)? Research question: A research question is the foundational, guiding inquiry that drives a research study. It articulates exactly what the researcher seeks to understand, explain, predict, or explore within the scope of a specific problem and purpose. It usually emerges after the problem has been identified and the purpose has been established. In essence, the research question translates the purpose into a focused, answerable inquiry. Hypothesis: A hypothesis is a clear, testable, and predictive statement about the expected relationship between two or more variables. It is formulated in response to a research question, particularly in quantitative research, and reflects a theoretical expectation based on prior evidence or reasoning. In essence, it’s a tentative answer to the research question—one that can be supported or refuted through systematic investigation. The yearlong investigation culminates in an academic paper of 4,000-5,000 words and a presentation with an oral defense. Students develop competencies in research methodology and design, ethical research practices, accessing, analyzing, and synthesizing information, and reflection and documentation of scholarly processes.

What others have discovered? ================================================================================ BROADER CONCEPTS AND TERMS ---------------------------------------- - Literature Review or Conceptual Framework SPECIFIC RESEARCH TERMS AND SUB-TOPICS ---------------------------------------- - Topical Research - Theoretical Framework - Problem Statement-Description of the problem - Historical Context/Foundational Knowledge DEFINITIONS AND KEY CONCEPTS ---------------------------------------- Conceptual Framework: A conceptual framework is both a process and a product through which a researcher constructs and presents the “why” of the study — that is, why the topic matters — and links that rationale to the literature, theory, methodology, and the design of the study. It serves as a mechanism for aligning and integrating the literature review, research questions, theoretical or conceptual underpinnings, methods, and analysis of the study Key Elements (based on Ravitch & Riggan, 2017) Here are the major elements or functions of a conceptual framework according to their presentation: -Concepts, assumptions, expectations, beliefs, and theories: The framework makes explicit the researcher’s underlying views about the phenomena under study. -Alignment and coherence: It shows how the study’s literature review, theoretical orientation, research questions, methods, data collection, and analysis are interconnected and coherent. -Guide and ballast: The framework acts as both a guide (steering the research) and ballast (providing stability) in the conduct of an empirical study. -Processes of development: It’s not only a document or diagram at the start, but a process of continual refinement as the study evolves. -Justification of research decisions: The framework assists in justifying why particular research methods, data collection strategies, and analytic approaches were selected over others. Literature Review: A literature review is the process and product of reviewing existing scholarship on relevant concepts, phenomena, contexts and studies related to your research topic. In their work, Ravitch & Riggan emphasize that a strong literature review: -Surveys what has already been written, identifies gaps or tensions in the body of w

es, and analytic approaches were selected over others. Literature Review: A literature review is the process and product of reviewing existing scholarship on relevant concepts, phenomena, contexts and studies related to your research topic. In their work, Ravitch & Riggan emphasize that a strong literature review: -Surveys what has already been written, identifies gaps or tensions in the body of work, and helps frame the importance of the study. -Is not simply a descriptive summary but provides a critical, analytical engagement with the literature — showing how prior research shapes your topic and how your study will contribute. -Serves as one of the components that feed into a conceptual framework; in particular it helps define the “topical research” component discussed below (i.e., what has been done, and what remains). -Helps justify your methodological choices by showing what has worked (or not) in previous work and by situating your research in that scholarly conversation. In summary, a literature review is the foundation that situates your study in the existing body of knowledge and creates a rationale for your work. Topical Research: Within the framework of Ravitch & Riggan, “topical research” refers to the body of work and empirical studies directly relevant to the topic you are studying — essentially the “what” of the study, as opposed to the “why” or “how.” They list “topical research” as one of the three primary elements of a conceptual framework (alongside personal interests and theoretical frameworks). Key points about topical research: -It embodies the empirical investigations, studies, and discussions already conducted in your substantive area (“what others have done”). -It helps you identify the constructs, variables, phenomena, or contexts that must be explored or challenged in your research. -The topical research component supports the argument for the study’s significance: by showing where prior work has focused, where gaps remain, and how your study fits in. -In their phrasing, Ravitch & Riggan distinguish that topical research “describes the what of the study” while theoretical frameworks clarify the why and how. In short: Topical research is the domain of prior empirical or topic‑specific literature that grounds the study’s focus and helps define what is being studied. Theoretical Frameworks: According to Ravitch & Riggan, the theoretic

udy fits in. -In their phrasing, Ravitch & Riggan distinguish that topical research “describes the what of the study” while theoretical frameworks clarify the why and how. In short: Topical research is the domain of prior empirical or topic‑specific literature that grounds the study’s focus and helps define what is being studied. Theoretical Frameworks: According to Ravitch & Riggan, the theoretical framework is a subset of the conceptual framework (but a critical one) that brings in formal theories, constructs, assumptions, and models to explain and interpret phenomena. It helps articulate the underpinning reasons and mechanisms — the “why” and “how” of the research. Key features of the theoretical framework: -It comprises linked concepts, assumptions, expectations, beliefs, and existing theories that the researcher uses to frame their study. -It guides the research design, informing what you will measure/observe, how you will interpret those observations, and how you will relate them back to theory. -It provides the lens through which you view your topic, and thus helps to locate your study within broader theoretical conversations. -While many authors treat theoretical and conceptual frameworks as interchangeable, Ravitch & Riggan argue that the theoretical framework is embedded within the broader conceptual framework (which also includes personal interests and topical research). In short: The theoretical framework is the structured set of theories and constructs that explains how and why the research topic is being approached in a particular way. Its core purposes are identifying and critiquing existing literature while exposing research gaps. Six Review Approaches: - Argumentative: Selectively examines literature to support or refute established arguments - Integrative: Reviews, critiques, and synthesizes representative literature generating new frameworks - Historical: Traces issue evolution across time periods - Methodological: Focuses on how research was conducted, examining analysis frameworks - Systematic: Uses pre-specified and standardized methods to identify and critically appraise relevant research - Theoretical: Examines accumulated theory regarding concepts or phenomena From AP Research Step 3 (Kennesaw State): Step 3 focuses on reviewing existing knowledge before designing your research study. A literature review serves three critical functions: identifying small gaps in existing knowledge, confirming the viability of a research question, and demonstrating the added value of your work. Three Strategic Components: - Position: Selecting a narrow topic and answerable research question - Justify: Confirming research viability and identifying knowledge gaps - Frame: Creating a coherent conceptual or theoretical framework

hree Strategic Components: - Position: Selecting a narrow topic and answerable research question - Justify: Confirming research viability and identifying knowledge gaps - Frame: Creating a coherent conceptual or theoretical framework

================================================================================ HOPSCOTCH STEP 4: Step 4: How will I study it? ================================================================================ BROADER CONCEPTS AND TERMS ---------------------------------------- - Qualitative Methodological Approach - Quantitative Methodological Approach - Mixed-Methods Approach - Other Designs (Data Agnostic) SPECIFIC RESEARCH TERMS AND SUB-TOPICS ---------------------------------------- - Research Design: Narrative, Phenomenology, Phenomenography, Ethnography, School Ethnography, Grounded theory, Case study research , Action research, Design-Based Research - Research Design: Descriptive design, Correlational design, Cross-sectional survey design, Pre-experimental design , Quasi-experimental design, Experimental design - Research Design: Convergent Parallel Design, Exploratory Sequential Design, Explanatory Sequential Design, Embedded Design - Research Design: Experimental, Formal, Design-based, Intervention, Descriptive, Performance design, Participatory, HCI methods, Algorithm design, Framework design, Theoretical proof, Clinical study" DEFINITIONS AND KEY CONCEPTS ---------------------------------------- Quantitative research gathers a range of numeric data, and uses a set of strategies, techniques, and assumptions to explore numeric patterns to investigate why something occurs. The researcher seeks to establish the overall tendency, to identify a trend, or to determine the differences among observed to explain the relationships among variables. Qualitative research is an approach to inquiry that seeks to understand how people make sense of their experiences, social worlds, and cultural meanings. It prioritizes depth over breadth, focusing on rich, contextualized data collected in natural settings. It is interpretive, inductive, and often constructivist or transformative in nature—meaning it assumes that reality is socially constructed and best understood through the perspectives of those who live it, and that it can be transformed through inquiry. Mixed-methods research is an approach to inquiry that integrates both quantitative (numeric, measurable) and qualitative (textual, contextual) data collection and analysis within a single study or research program. It is used when neither quantitative nor qualitative methods alone are sufficient to fully understa

he perspectives of those who live it, and that it can be transformed through inquiry. Mixed-methods research is an approach to inquiry that integrates both quantitative (numeric, measurable) and qualitative (textual, contextual) data collection and analysis within a single study or research program. It is used when neither quantitative nor qualitative methods alone are sufficient to fully understand a research problem. At its core, mixed-methods research is pragmatic—it values methodological pluralism and chooses the approach (or combination of approaches) best suited to answering the research questions. Experiments allow researchers to make causal inferences. Random assignment is critical—participants are placed into conditions by chance rather than choice. Correlational Research involves passive observation and measurement without intervention. Researchers identify patterns between variables but cannot determine causation. A key limitation: correlation does not mean causation. Quasi-Experimental Designs resemble experiments but lack random assignment. Instead, researchers use existing group memberships as independent variables. Longitudinal Studies track the same individuals over extended periods, from weeks to decades. Qualitative Designs include participant observation, case studies, and narrative analysis. Surveys gather information through questionnaires or online methods, reaching large populations cost-effectively. From Deakin University (Qualitative Study Designs): A qualitative study design is a research method for understanding complex, contextual, and subjective phenomena. Qualitative research prioritizes depth and insight, addressing why and how questions rather than frequency counts. Eight qualitative methodologies: phenomenology, grounded theory, ethnography, his

ng large populations cost-effectively. From Deakin University (Qualitative Study Designs): A qualitative study design is a research method for understanding complex, contextual, and subjective phenomena. Qualitative research prioritizes depth and insight, addressing why and how questions rather than frequency counts. Eight qualitative methodologies: phenomenology, grounded theory, ethnography, historical research, narrative inquiry, action research, case studies, and field research. Five primary data collection methods: focus groups, observation, document analysis, surveys/questionnaires, and interviews.

================================================================================ HOPSCOTCH STEP 5: Step 5: What is my research question? ================================================================================ BROADER CONCEPTS AND TERMS ---------------------------------------- - Research Question /Hypothesis /Issue SPECIFIC RESEARCH TERMS AND SUB-TOPICS ---------------------------------------- - Qualitative research question - Quantitative research - aim+hypothesis - Mixed Methods research: Qualitative questions and Quantitative aim+hypothesis - Other structures of the question DEFINITIONS AND KEY CONCEPTS ---------------------------------------- Research Question: The question that the study or research project aims to answer (Bouchrika, 2025). Addresses a specific issue or problem that can be answered in the study’s conclusion (Bouchrika, 2025). Outlines various aspects of the study, including the population, variables to be studied, and the problem the study addresses (Bouchrika, 2025). Comes from a gap in the research or conflicting research results in literature (Creswell, 2014). States the specific issue or problem that the study or research project will focus on (Monash University, 2025) The question around which the study or research project is centered around (George Mason University Writing Center, 2024) Hypothesis: Predictions about what the results of the study or research project will show and is usually about the relationship between the variables (Creswell, 2014). Developed by reviewing existing evidence and then inferring what could happen within the specific context of the study or research project (Price et al., 2018) Can be tested through empirical investigation Two different types of hypotheses include alternative and null hypothesis (Creswell, 2014). Alternative hypotheses specify the results or relationships that are expected between the variables. Null hypotheses indicate no expected difference or no relationship between the variables. Issue: A research issue in qualitative research refers to the broad area of concern, tension, or interest that prompts inquiry. It is the starting point—a real-world problem, social phenomenon, or area of ambiguity—that the researcher wants to explore in depth. Unlike a tightly defined problem in quantitative research, a qualitative research issue is often open-ended, complex, and interpretive. I

Issue: A research issue in qualitative research refers to the broad area of concern, tension, or interest that prompts inquiry. It is the starting point—a real-world problem, social phenomenon, or area of ambiguity—that the researcher wants to explore in depth. Unlike a tightly defined problem in quantitative research, a qualitative research issue is often open-ended, complex, and interpretive. It reflects something worth understanding rather than solving or measuring. " Qualitative research question: Linked to the research design/tradition while being adaptable, non-directional, and more flexible than quantitative research questions (Creswell, 2014). Two different forms of qualitative research questions are central question and associated subquestions (Creswell, 2014). Central research question is broad and focuses on exploring the central phenomenon or concept in the study. Subquestions assists in narrowing the focus of the study while leaving it open for varied perspectives or meanings. Begins with words such as how or what and use exploratory verbs, such as explore, understand, or discover. Quantitative research question: Inquire about the relationships between variables that the research study is investigating (Creswell, 2014). Develops a link between the research question and the research design (Bouchrika, 2025). Usually categorized into three different types: descriptive, comparative, and relationship (Bouchrika, 2025). Descriptive research questions try to measure the responses of the research study’s population to one or more variables or describe the variables the research will measure. Typically begins with what. Comparative research questions try to discover the differences between two or more groups for an outcome variable. Example: Comparing a group who received the treatment vs. another group that did not. Relationship research questions try to explore and define trends and interactions between two or more variables. Typically use words such as association or trends as the question includes both independent and dependent variables. Mixed methods research question: Directly addresses the mixing of the quantitative and qualitative strands of the research (Creswell, 2014). Known as hybrid or integrated question (Tashakkori & Creswell, 2007). Emphasize the methods or the content of the study or both (Creswell, 2014). INTERNAL HOPSCOTCH RESOURCES

DEFINITIONS AND KEY CONCEPTS ---------------------------------------- Analysis Organizing and interpreting the data to figure out what can be learned and make sense of what the data is displaying (Glesne, 2016). Describe, compare, create explanations, link data to other data, possibly pose hypotheses, develop theories, or conclusions. Level 2 - Narrower Concepts/Te

s, Histogram, Bar graphs, other. DEFINITIONS AND KEY CONCEPTS ---------------------------------------- Analysis Organizing and interpreting the data to figure out what can be learned and make sense of what the data is displaying (Glesne, 2016). Describe, compare, create explanations, link data to other data, possibly pose hypotheses, develop theories, or conclusions. Level 2 - Narrower Concepts/Terms (Qualitative Data Analysis) Coding (Glesne, 2016): Progressive process of sorting and defining data into clumps labeled with a code to create a thematic organizational framework. Links thoughts and actions across bits of data. Discern themes, patterns, and processes to compare and build theoretical explanations. Memoing (Glesne, 2016): Informal notes taken by the researcher to track thoughts and ideas, possibly recorded. Data Saturation (Glesne, 2016): Successive examination of sources yields redundancy and completeness. Content Analysis (Hsieh & Shannon, 2005): Subjective interpretation of text data through systematic coding to identify themes or patterns. Thematic Analysis (Glesne, 2016): Searches for themes and patterns in data. Triangulation (Glesne, 2016): Use of multiple methods to validate findings. Quantitative Data Analysis: Descriptive statistics (Coolidge, 2021): Measuring data using graphs, tables, and descriptions such as means and averages; parameters include mean and standard deviation. Inferential statistics (Coolidge, 2021): Making inferences about a population from a sample. Mixed Methods Data Analysis (Creswell, 2014): Quantitative and qualitative data are analyzed separately then merged for comparison or transformation, often using joint displays or side-by-side results. Level 3 - Related Concepts/Terms Qualitative Data Analysis: Open coding – breaking data into parts and labeling concepts; Axial coding – connecting categories and subcategories; Selective coding – identifying the core category integrating all findings. Quantitative Data Analysis: Measures of central tendency (mean, median, mode); Measures of dispersion (range, variance, standard deviation, interquartile range); Measures of relative position (percentiles, quartiles, z-scores). Boxplots, tables, scatter plots, histograms, and bar graphs visualize data distribution. Inferential statistics: T-tests compare means; Correlation measures relationships (r value); ANOVA compares means

sures of central tendency (mean, median, mode); Measures of dispersion (range, variance, standard deviation, interquartile range); Measures of relative position (percentiles, quartiles, z-scores). ================================================================================ HOPSCOTCH STEP 8: Step 8: How will I ensure the evidence is trustworthy? ================================================================================ BROADER CONCEPTS AND TERMS ---------------------------------------- - Trustworthiness - Qualitative Research - Validity & Reliability - Quantitative Research SPECIFIC RESEARCH TERMS AND SUB-TOPICS ---------------------------------------- - Trustworthiness in qualitative research includes but no limited by: Credibility, Transferability , Dependability and Confirmability. - Validity in quantitative research includes several types such us: Internal and external Validity/ generalizability based on sampling (random sampling, stratified sampling, cluster sampling, convenience sampling, accidental sampling) and construct and content validity. - Reliability in quantitative research is expressed through: Test- retest reliability, inter- rate reliability, internal consistency. DEFINITIONS AND KEY CONCEPTS ---------------------------------------- Level 1 - Broader Concepts/Terms: The integrity and ethical aspects of the process in conducting the research. Researchers need to protect their research participants, develop trust with them; promote the integrity of research; guard against misconduct and impropriety that might reflect on their organizations or institutions; and cope with new, challenging problems (Israel & Hay, 2006, as cited in Creswell, 2014). Level 2 - Narrower Concepts/Terms: Qualitative Research - Credibility: Refers to the truthfulness and believability of findings from the participants' perspective. Ensures the study accurately represents the lived experiences or meanings shared by participants (e.g., prolonged engagement, triangulation, member checking, peer debriefing). Transferability: Addresses the extent to which findings can apply to other contexts through rich, thick description (e.g., detailed contextualization of participants, setting, and findings). Dependability: Focuses on the stability and consistency of the research process over time (e.g., maintaining an audit trail). Confirmability: Ensures findings reflect participants’ experiences, not researcher bias (e.g., audit trails, reflexive journaling, triangulation). Quantitative Research - Validity: The accuracy and integrity of the research—whether it measure

ipants, setting, and findings). Dependability: Focuses on the stability and consistency of the research process over time (e.g., maintaining an audit trail). Confirmability: Ensures findings reflect participants’ experiences, not researcher bias (e.g., audit trails, reflexive journaling, triangulation). Quantitative Research - Validity: The accuracy and integrity of the research—whether it measures what it claims to measure. Includes internal validity (causal inference), external validity (generalizability), construct validity (accurate definition and measurement), and content validity (comprehensive coverage of concepts). Reliability: The consistency and stability of measurement. Includes test-retest reliability (over time), inter-rater reliability (between observers), and internal consistency (e.g., Cronbach’s alpha). Sampling: The process of selecting participants to ensure representativeness and support generalizability and unbiased estimation. ================================================================================ HOPSCOTCH STEP 9: Step 9 -How will I be ethical and safe in my study? ================================================================================ BROADER CONCEPTS AND TERMS ---------------------------------------- - Integrity and Ethics in conducting research SPECIFIC RESEARCH TERMS AND SUB-TOPICS ---------------------------------------- - IRB approval Respect for persons Beneficence Justice DEFINITIONS AND KEY CONCEPTS ---------------------------------------- Level 1 - Broader Concepts/Terms: The moral integrity of the researcher is a critically important aspect of ensuring that the research process and the researcher’s findings are trustworthy and valid (Hesse-Biber, 2006). The ethical principles driving a study, whether quantitative, qualitative, or mixed, are closely tied to its standards for credibility and validity. Researchers must protect participants, build trust, maintain integrity, prevent misconduct, and navigate ethical challenges (Israel & Hay, 2006, as cited in Creswell, 2014). Level 2 - Narrower Concepts/Terms: Respect for persons involves protecting individual autonomy and ensuring informed consent. People should be treated as autonomous and given full information to decide about participation (AVAC, 2025). People with diminished autonomy (e.g., children, the ill, or cognitively impaired) should be specially protected and only included under specific, ethical conditions. Beneficence requires doing no harm while maximizing benefits and minimizing risks to participants (AVAC, 2025). Justice refers to fair participant recruitment, trial location, and distribution of research burdens and benefits (AVAC, 2025). Level 3 - Related Concepts/Terms: Do No Harm – Ensure no participants are harmed. Stop the study if adverse effects are noticed. Privacy & Anonymity – Protect participants' identities and seek permission before disclosure. Confidentiality – Maintain secrecy unless emergency conditions require otherwise; handle minors' data with extra care. Informed Consent – Participants must know the study’s nature and voluntarily consent. The consent form should include researcher and institution ID, purpose, benefits, involvement, risks, confidentiality, right to withdraw, and contacts (Sarantakos, 2005, as cited in Creswell, 2014). Rapport & Friendship – Establish pro

s emergency conditions require otherwise; handle minors' data with extra care. Informed Consent – Participants must know the study’s nature and voluntarily consent. The consent form should include researcher and institution ID, purpose, benefits, involvement, risks, confidentiality, right to withdraw, and contacts (Sarantakos, 2005, as cited in Creswell, 2014). Rapport & Friendship – Establish professional rapport without creating false perceptions of friendship. Intrusiveness – Researchers should not intrude excessively into participants’ lives. Inappropriate Behavior – Avoid personal or romantic entanglements with participants; maintain ethical boundaries. Data Interpretation – Analyze data truthfully and ensure credibility. Data Ownership & Rewards – Researchers usually own their work but may consider ethical sharing if there are significant gains.
